This paper describes AegisOT, a single Zero-Trust security gateway, which offers cross-protocol security to both DNP3 and OPC UA traffic without needing any modifications to the deployed field equipment. The framework combines both time validation with a Redis store, perpoint Access Control Lists (ACLs) and Merkle-chained audit logging to support tamper-evident forensics. The implementation of AegisOT is a containerized microservice deployed to a simulated OT environment. It has been found that malicious commands are effectively detected and blocked with low latency overhead. The suggested solution provides a vendor-free security layer that is practical in increasing resiliency in industrial communication.

title: Enhancing Movie Recommendations: A Comparative Study of Filtering Techniques
authors: Srinivasan Arindham, Narayanan Anirudh, Christopher Colombus C
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Sustainable Computing and Smart Systems (ICSCSS)
abstract: The exponential growth of digital movie content has intensified the challenge of delivering personalized recommendations that truly reflect individual user preferences. This study presents a comprehensive comparative analysis of multiple filtering techniques—collaborative, popularity-based, and random filtering—within the context of movie recommendation systems. By leveraging advanced feature extraction methods such as CountVectorizer and similarity metrics like Cosine Similarity, the research constructs robust movie representations from enriched metadata, including genres, cast, crew, and keywords. The proposed hybrid framework integrates structured data preprocessing, scalable algorithmic orchestration, and external API integration to enhance both the precision and diversity of recommendations. Evaluation is conducted across a broad spectrum of metrics, demonstrating that the hybrid approach not only improves accuracy and novelty but also addresses persistent challenges such as cold-start scenarios and data sparsity. The findings offer actionable insights for designing effective, user-centric recommendation systems that optimize the movie discovery experience while maintaining computational efficiency and adaptability.

title: Front Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS)

title: A Hybrid Drift-Aware Causal and Counterfactual Explainability Framework for Transparent Loan Default Prediction
authors: N. Srujana Roy, Seemakurthy Hupesh Naga Ketan, Mohebbanaaz
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)
abstract: Banks and lending organisations often rely on models to predict which borrowers may default on loans. Many of these models mainly focus on improving accuracy, but they do not always explain how decisions are made or stay reliable when the data changes over time. In real lending situations, both loan features and borrower behaviour keep changing, which can reduce model performance and sometimes result in confusing or misleading explanations. In this work, we describe an approach for loan default prediction that uses explainable machinelearning methods, causal analysis, and data-drift monitoring. The system includes Population Stability Index-based drift detection, Average Treatment Effect to study causal influence, SHAP values for feature importance, and DiCE to generate “what-if” style explanations for individual borrowers. An XGBoost model is used for prediction. The approach is tested on a realistic synthetic loan dataset that includes time-based features, so the model can be evaluated without using real customer data. The results show that the system can detect changes in data patterns, identify key factors linked to default, and generate useful counterfactual recommendations. Overall, this study shows how different explanation methods can be combined in one workflow, placing more importance on clarity and reliability rather than accuracy alone.

title: The Social Amplification of AI Acceptance: Mapping Attitude Formation and Purchase Intention in Intelligent Retail Environments
authors: Philip Antonius Budianto, Brandon Jhon, Adele B L Mailangkay
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)
abstract: This paper will review the effect of social amplification processes on consumer attitudes toward artificial intelligence (AI) and purchase intention in intelligent retail settings. It deals with the little knowledge about the social and psychological mechanisms that underpin AI acceptance and the role that these factors play in influencing attitude development and purchase decision-making among consumers in the present day. The study will chart the association among social influence, consumer perceptions, and purchase intention and offer empirical evidence on consumer readiness to do business with AI-driven retail technologies. The survey was administered in a structured questionnaire in October 2025, with 252 valid responses. The sample is predominantly made of young, tech-savvy people, with the 40.9% within the 26-30 age bracket, the 32.9% within the 17-25 age bracket, and the 25.4 within the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$31-40$</tex> age bracket, and the 55.6 within the Jakarta (Jabodetabek) region. High school graduates (51.2 percent) and undergraduates (43.3 percent) are the most common educational backgrounds of the respondents who belong to diverse occupational groups. The results indicate that the interplay of social cues, perceived usefulness, trust, and the contextual characteristics of intelligent retail systems influence the attitudes towards AI. More favorable attitudes cause a major increase in purchase intention which is why the primary importance of social aspects in strengthening AI acceptance and provides a strategic platform that effectively optimizes the adoption of intelligent technology in the retail market.

title: Formal IoT Device Recruitment Architecture and Case Study
authors: Mubarak Mohammad, Fatima Abdallah, Hussein Othman
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Multidisciplinary Conference on Engineering Technology (IMCET)
abstract: This paper extends our recently published architecture for automated IoT device recruitment and service provisioning using smart contracts [1]. While the initial work established the rationale, motivation, and high-level architecture, this paper deepens the contribution by providing a formal foundation and comprehensive case study validation. We present a detailed formal specification of the smart contract service components, decomposing them into functional, non-functional, and contractual parts with precise logical expressions. The architecture is further formalized using Web Ontology Language (OWL), enabling machine-processable modeling and validation. A rigorous framework for completeness and correctness verification at three critical stages (before publication, before execution, and after execution) is defined. To demonstrate practical applicability, we implement a case study on autonomous train gate control at road intersections, showcasing the architecture’s effectiveness in a real-world IoT scenario. The integration of formal methods with blockchain-based automation provides a verifiable foundation for trusted IoT service deployment in dynamic environments.

title: Short-Term Residential Load and Solar Power Forecasting Using Machine Learning
authors: Nayab Sohail Nadeem, Habiba Ahmed, Mohamad Nassereddine
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Multidisciplinary Conference on Engineering Technology (IMCET)
abstract: The increasing penetration of rooftop photovoltaic systems and battery storage in residential buildings has created a growing need for accurate short-term forecasting of electricity consumption and solar power generation. Reliable forecasts at short horizons are essential for reducing grid dependence, improving battery operation, and enabling intelligent energy management systems. This paper presents a machine-learning-based framework for short-term forecasting of residential load demand and rooftop photovoltaic generation using high-resolution five-minute real-world data from a hybrid grid-connected system. Forecasts are generated for horizons ranging from fifteen to sixty minutes ahead and evaluated against a persistence-based baseline. Ridge Regression, Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting models are developed and compared using standard error metrics. Results show that machine-learning models consistently outperform the baseline. For load forecasting, RMSE reductions of approximately 19% at a 60-minute horizon are achieved, with normalized errors of about 28 W per minute. For photovoltaic generation, improvements exceed 25%, with errors as low as 15 W per minute at 60 minutes. These findings support the use of machine learning for real-time residential energy management, improving battery scheduling and solar utilization.

title: Boundary Conditions of Cost-Aware Active Learning: A Multi-Dataset Taxonomy of Calibration and Length-Variance Failure Modes
authors: Vihanga Supasan Kariyakaranage, Banuka Athuraliya
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)
abstract: Active Learning (AL) strategies that incorporate annotation cost, such as the recently proposed CAL-Log heuristic, have demonstrated significant efficiency gains in verbose, document-heavy Data Mining and Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks. However, the stability of cost-aware selection remains poorly understood across the diverse spectrum of linguistic regimes. This paper presents a systematic diagnostic study of CAL-Log's performance boundaries across ten diverse text classification benchmarks, ranging from short-form social media tweets to long-form cinematic reviews. Our analysis reveals that the utility of cost-normalization is governed by a “Variance Law”: substantial gains are realized only when document length variance (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sigma_{L}$</tex>) exceeds a critical threshold (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sigma_{L} \approx 15$</tex>), below which the acquisition engine undergoes “Selection Collapse” and introduces stochastic noise. Furthermore, we identify a “Calibration Trap”—a phenomenon where high Expected Calibration Error (ECE) in high-cardinality tasks causes the learner to prioritize “cheaply wrong” samples, actively undermining the cost-utility tradeoff. We provide a comprehensive taxonomy of these failure modes and introduce a metadata-driven practitioner's decision framework for selecting optimal acquisition strategies. Our findings establish the first empirical boundary conditions for deploying cost-aware active learning in production NLP pipelines.

title: VANETs Using Onion Routing with ML Based Message Authentication
authors: Nikhita Devi V, Nethra Bharathwaj, Y Moksha, Rajeswari D
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: Vehicular Ad Hoc Networks (VANETs) need to have solid security measures in place to promote the reliability and privacy of communications in a crucially dynamic setup. In this paper, a multi-layered VANET security framework will be proposed, which combines NS-3-based simulation, machine learning-based intrusion detection, and onion routing with hybrid encryption. Realistic VANET simulation is used to generate traffic in the network with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 0 - 5 0}$</tex> nodes using IEEE 802.11b and OLSR routing. Several attack conditions such as flooding, black-hole attacks as well as Sybil attacks are injected to produce labeled datasets. The malicious messages are detected using a machine learning gateway which employs the network level features of a message to identify the type of message as malicious using the classifiers of Logistic Regression, Random Forest and Support Vector Machine. Validated messages are only transmitted to a privacy-preserving onion routing module which uses RSA-2048 as key exchange and AES-256 as layers encryption. Packet-level analysis by Wireshark confirms the presence of secure end-to-end communication, as well as the ability to credit the presence of successful attack filtering. The proposed integrated framework can be demonstrated to be robust as the results of the experiments indicate high detection accuracy and effective privacy preservation.

title: GenAI-Empowered Blended Learning: Model Reconstruction and Quasi-Experimental Validation
authors: Hailang Chen, Yingxia Ye
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI in Education Technology and Applications (AIETA)
abstract: To address the structural predicaments of lagging data processing, imbalanced resource allocation, and superficial interactions in traditional blended learning, a Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI)-empowered blended learning model was constructed. A non-equivalent control group pretestposttest quasi-experimental design was adopted, with two classes (control: n=51; experimental: n=54) from an Embedded Technology major undergoing a 16-week “Fundamentals of Artificial Intelligence” course experiment. Results indicate that the experimental group significantly outperformed the control group in overall achievement, practical projects, and processoriented assessments (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p}<0.01$</tex>), with pronounced advantages in higher-order competencies. Longitudinal tracking revealed a three-stage evolutionary pattern: initial convergence, mid-term differentiation, and late-stage stabilization. The model effectively propels the paradigm shift of blended learning toward data-intelligence-driven modalities.

title: Optimized Lung Cancer Classification Using HybridNet
authors: Kurdeepan P, Tadigotla Bhuvan Vijay, Manjula J
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: Lung cancer is one of the main causes of cancer-related death. Given that early detection is a critical factor, one of the greatest challenges associated with lung cancer is its impact on overall survival rates. The potential significance of CT scans with respect to the early identification of this disease should not be overlooked. Currently, the process of identifying lung cancers through CT imaging is a manual analysis of images, and consequently, there is considerable variability due to the subjectivity of interpretation, which can lead to inaccuracies in the results. To address this, HybridNet is proposed as a two-armed hybrid deep learning framework that uses VGG16 and InceptionV3 as dual paths for CT-based binary lung cancer classification. The results show that HybridNet uses two convolutional neural networks with transfer learning to extract hierarchical and multi-scale feature representations, and concatenation improves results while maintaining computational efficiency. The experimental evaluation demonstrates that Hy bridNet performs with an accuracy of 99.14%, precision of 96.52%, and a recall rate of 99.90%, exceeding both VGG16 and InceptionV3 as independent models. Moreover, confusion matrix evaluations demonstrate strong true positive detection rates and minimal false positive determinations. These findings show that hybrid deep learning models can be implemented as a reliable and feasible method for lung cancer screening with important implications for medical imaging analysis.

title: An Embedded Wearable System for Running Performance Analysis Using Temporal Effort Modelling
authors: Harish Sekar, Arvind Krishna R, Shrinivass C, Nithya V
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: Running performance is affected by the time-evolving process of physiological effort, especially during pacing transition and fatigue states; nevertheless, conventional wearables measure instantaneous physiological or motion-related features that do not consider the time-evolving process. To overcome the limitations of conventional wearables, This paper proposes an embedded wrist-worn wearable system for running performance analysis using the time-evolving process of physiological effort. In our proposed system, photoplethysmography heart rate monitoring, inertial motion measurement, and galvanic skin response measurement are used to measure cardiovascular, biomechanical, and stress-related features. In our proposed system, the features are extracted using the sliding window method and are processed using a lightweight temporal CNN (1D CNN). The model is trained using publicly available inertial measurement datasets and is implemented on the ESP32 microcontroller using TinyML for Microcontrollers. In our proposed system, the accuracy of effort zone classification is found to be 92 % using real-time data collected using the proposed prototype.

title: RAG-HA: Recognizing AI-Generated Handwritten Answers based on Argument-Mining Framework
authors: Avijit Gayen, Sumana Sikdar, Anwesha Bose, Anwesha De, Arunima Mandal, Angshuman Jana
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)
abstract: This paper presents RAG-HA, a dual-pipeline framework for detecting AI-generated content in digitized handwritten answers by integrating argument-mining-inspired linguistic analysis with transformer-based semantic modeling. The first pipeline extracts lightweight, interpretable features including average sentence length, vocabulary richness, readability score, argument density, word count, and GPT-2 perplexity and applies a 300 -tree Random Forest classifier to support transparent decision-making. The second pipeline encodes responses using multiple sentence-transformer embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, all-mpnet-bAs a result, the traditional purpose of examinations is being challenged, and the underlying meaning is no longer the same as earlier.ers. Experimental results on 280 annotated responses for training and 70 unseen test samples show that transformer-based representations consistently outperform feature-only baselines, with the MiniLM + K-Nearest Neighbors configuration achieving the best performance (92.86% accuracy, AUC 0.93). The findings demonstrate that integrating interpretable linguistic cues with robust semantic representations provides an effective and scalable foundation for detecting AIgenerated handwritten academic responses.

title: Intelligent Control of a DC-DC Buck-Boost Converter Using ANN to Enhance Voltage Regulation
authors: Ramprabu J, Kavishree S, Nandhakumar K, Lokesh R, Suganya R
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS)
abstract: In recent years, intelligent control methods have achieved a lot of interest in power electronic converters with their capability of addressing nonlinearity of the system and dynamic operating conditions. An Artificial Neural Network (ANN)-based control strategy of a DC-DC boost converter is proposed in this paper that would assist in improving voltage regulation and minimizing output variations. The controller suggested is dynamically controlled to change the duty cycle of the converter switch using real-time voltage and current control feedback. The MATLAB is used to model and simulate the entire system. The use of simulation shows that ANN controlled converters attain quicker settling time, minimal voltage ripple, and stable current reaction in various conditions. The results that were obtained show that the proposed approach will be suitable in the context of renewable energy and electric vehicles power management.

title: Smart Adaptive Wearable System for Multi-Modal Muscle Recovery Stimulation
authors: Aswin VanithaKandasamy, Joshua Paul Kiruban M, Amit Kumar, Aruna Priya P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: Post-exercise muscle recovery is a persistent challenge for athletes, often causing prolonged discomfort and delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS). Existing methods like ice packs and foam rolling lack personalization and real-time feedback. This paper presents a smart wearable recovery device that uses targeted heat and vibration therapy to enhance localized blood circulation and accelerate recovery. The system integrates an EMG sensor to measure muscle fatigue, an IMU to regulate vibration intensity, and a temperature sensor for safe thermal delivery. Together, these enable adaptive, closed-loop control across multiple therapy modes suited to different recovery phases. Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) connectivity allows users to monitor data and adjust settings via a companion application. This noninvasive, sensor-driven approach offers a personalized alternative to conventional recovery methods, with promising potential to reduce recovery time and advance wearable health technology in sports rehabilitation.

title: Optimization of Energy Management System with Different Operating Strategies for Greenhouse Heating: A Case Study in Lebanon
authors: Loubna Khaled, Raymond Ghaleb, Rabih Dib, Bakri Abdulhay, Nazih Moubayed
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Multidisciplinary Conference on Engineering Technology (IMCET)
abstract: Sustainability in agriculture involves protected cultivation systems to create a controlled environment for crop growth, particularly in the face of climate change. Year-round production in Lebanon requires an effective energy management system to sustain essential operations. This study examines combinations of electric generation methods to meet the analytically modeled heating load to maintain an 18 °C internal temperature, during winter, for 20 standard Quonset greenhouses on a 10,000 m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> area of land. Operating strategies include a single diesel generator, with and without heat recovery, dual diesel generators, photovoltaic panels with different layouts (South and East/West), and battery storage. Each strategy is modeled and examined under various operating conditions in 2023. The multi-objective Pareto optimization, with a weighted average approach, is based on the normalized factors: the Levelized Cost of Energy, Renewable Fraction, and payback period, providing an adequate choice given the available land area. Each strategy offers specific economic, environmental, or spatial benefits. The results indicate that fully removing the diesel generator is currently unfeasible for heating applications. The strategy with the highest rank, with equal weights, is the dual diesel generators, followed by the hybrid with south-facing panels, which is the highest with 60% and more importance to the environment. This study provides an optimization tool for an energy management system for a greenhouse farm according to desired economic, environmental, or spatial parameters.

title: Exploration and Development of Electrolytic Water Catalysts Aided by Computational Chemistry
authors: Runtai Wang
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI in Education Technology and Applications (AIETA)
abstract: With growing global population, energy and environmental challenges have become increasingly urgent. Electrolysis of water, as an eco-friendly method for hydrogen production, has attracted significant attention. In this process, the efficiency of catalysts critically impacts the overall cost. Current catalysts are predominantly based on precious metals, which are expensive and still leave considerable room for improvement in catalytic performance. Therefore, developing highly efficient and low-cost catalysts is of great importance. Computational chemistry plays a pivotal role in advancing electrocatalyst research by predicting, guiding, and elucidating reaction mechanisms through theoretical calculations of energy changes and thermodynamic properties. This approach has significantly streamlined the synthesis and screening of catalysts. Such as Hydrogenation Catalysts for Pharmaceutical Production, CO2 Reduction Catalysts and Biomass Conversion Catalysts. Looking ahead, the integration of computational chemistry with artificial intelligence promises to further enhance the accuracy and speed of parameter calculations, enabling more precise predictions of catalyst performance and more efficient mechanistic insights. This could include automating the discovery of reaction pathways or tuning the active sites of catalysts for better efficiency.

title: Network Management of Multi-Vendor Devices: An LLM-Assisted Framework
authors: Zong-Yi Cai, Pei-Hsuan Hsieh
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)
abstract: Managing multi-vendor network devices remains challenging due to heterogeneous configuration interfaces, device models, and operational constraints. Although model-driven management approaches provide vendor-neutral abstractions, their effective adoption still requires substantial expertise in YANG models and configuration semantics. This study proposes an LLM-assisted framework that integrates OpenConfig-based abstraction with gNMI-driven state retrieval and configuration deployment to support effective management of multi-vendor networks. The framework employs collaborative large-language-model (LLM) reasoning to translate high-level user intents into OpenConfig-compliant configurations. To ensure accuracy and operational feasibility, a multi-stage verification pipeline is introduced, combining YANG schema validation using Yangson with simulation-based deployment in an emulated network environment. A prototype is then implemented to evaluate the performance of the proposed framework using a multi-vendor simulation setup built on EVE-NG. The results demonstrate the framework's feasibility in reducing operational complexity while maintaining vendor neutrality. The framework therefore offers a practical approach to incorporating LLMs into model-driven network-management workflows.

title: Impact of Conductive Filament Rupture Mechanisms in RRAM on Random Telegraph Noise and Its Applications
authors: Dejiang Mu, Han Lu, Pan Liu, Yujian Zhong, Xiangshui Miao, Xingsheng Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: During the RESET process of resistive random-access memory (RRAM), the high stochasticity of conductive filament (CF) rupture exacerbates multi—bit read errors. Through 3D multi-physics simulations, this study shows that the rupture—gap location shifts with rising RESET voltage. Experimental and simulation results both confirm that this positional variation modulates the amplitude of random telegraph noise (RTN) current fluctuations. Building on these findings, we propose a method to reduce inter—bit read errors by suppressing RTN fluctuations.

title: Wafer-Level 1/f Noise Characterization of Scaled P-Type Nanosheets and Comparison with Planar HKMG Transistors
authors: Ruben Asanovski, Anabela Veloso, Ben Kaczer, Naoto Horiguchi, Jacopo Franco
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: We present a comprehensive wafer-level 1/f noise study of scaled Gate-All-Around (GAA) p-type nanosheet transistors and benchmark them against large-area planar High- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\kappa$</tex> Metal Gate (HKMG) devices to assess the impact of device architecture on reliability. By statistically analyzing noise data from 188 nanosheets, we extract the effective trap density in the dielectric and compare it to that of planar transistors with identical gate stack, comparable processing thermal budgets, and no specific reliability anneals. The effective trap density extracted from 1/f noise is comparable in planar and nanosheet architectures. This indicates that transitioning to GAA nanosheets does not increase noise, which remains governed by gate stack quality.

title: PQC-Enhanced QKD Networks: A Layered Approach
authors: Paul Spooren, Andreas Neuhold, Sebastian Ramacher, Thomas Hühn
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We present a layered and modular network architecture that combines Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) and Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) to provide scalable end-to-end security across long distance multi-hop, trusted-node quantum networks. To ensure interoperability and efficient practical deployment, hop-wise tunnels between physically secured nodes are protected by WireGuard with periodically rotated pre-shared keys sourced via the ETSI GS QKD 014 interface. On top, Rosenpass performs a PQC key exchange to establish an end-to-end data channel without modifying deployed QKD devices or network protocols. This dual-layer composition yields post-quantum forward secrecy and authenticity under practical assumptions. We implement the design using open-source components and validate and evaluate it in simulated and lab test-beds. Experiments show uninterrupted operation over multihop paths, low resource footprint and fail-safe mechanisms. We further discuss the design's compositional security, wherein the security of each individual component is preserved under their combination and outline migration paths for operators integrating QKD-aware overlays in existing infrastructures.

title: Research on Multi-Mode Optimal Scheduling of Microgrids Based on Improved Multi-Objective Particle Swarm Optimization Algorithm
authors: Jinguo Li, Shihong Gan
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Aiming at the collaborative optimization problem of economy, environmental friendliness, and power supply reliability of microgrids under grid-connected/off-grid operation modes, an improved multi-objective particle swarm optimization algorithm with an adaptive inertia weight is proposed. An optimization model with operation cost, environmental protection cost, and power shortage rate as the objectives is established. The adaptive inertia weight is used to improve the optimization performance of the algorithm, thus achieving the multi-objective collaborative optimization of microgrids. Simulation results show that the proposed algorithm can obtain a uniformly distributed Pareto optimal solution set under both operation modes, which can provide effective support for microgrid operation decision-making.

title: Instruction-Set Architecture for Programmable NV-Center Quantum Repeater Nodes
authors: Vinay Kumar, Claudio Cicconetti, Riccardo Bassoli, Marco Conti, Andrea Passarella
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Programmability is increasingly central in emerging quantum network software stacks, yet the node-internal controller-to-hardware interface for quantum repeater devices remains under-specified. We introduce the idea of an instruction-set architecture (ISA) for controller-driven programmability of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center quantum repeater nodes. Each node consists of an optically interfaced electron spin acting as a data qubit and a long-lived nuclear-spin register acting as a control program. We formalize two modes of programmability: (i) deterministic register control, where the nuclear register is initialized in a basis state to select a specific operation on the data qubit; and (ii) coherent register control, where the register is prepared in superposition, enabling coherent combinations of operations beyond classical programmability. Network protocols are expressed as controller-issued instruction vectors, which we illustrate through a compact realization of the BBPSSW purification protocol. We further show that coherent register control enables interferometric diagnostics such as fidelity witnessing and calibration, providing tools unavailable in classical programmability. Finally, we discuss scalability to multi-electron and multi-nuclear spin architectures and connection to Linear combination of unitaries (LCU) and Kraus formulation.

title: Quantum Random Forest for the Regression Problem
authors: Kamil Khadiev, Liliya Safina
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The Random Forest model is one of the popular models of Machine learning. We present a quantum algorithm for testing (forecasting) process of the Random Forest machine learning model for the Regression problem. The presented algorithm is more efficient (in terms of query complexity or running time) than the classical counterpart.

title: From Cornfields to Canyons: Route-Specific, Physics-Based, Predictive Reliability Modeling of DC–DC Power Converters
authors: Mir Md Fahimul Islam, Mikhail Lopes, Jabir Bin Jahangir, Woongkul Lee, Muhammad A. Alam
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: The reliability of power electronic converters remains a key bottleneck in electric vehicle (EV) adoption, directly affecting drivetrain longevity and total cost of ownership. Conventional lifetime models evaluate components independently under static thermal conditions, overlooking the coupled and time-varying stresses that dominate real-world operation. In this work, a physics-based degradation framework is developed and experimentally calibrated to capture three layers of a realistic application: (i) coupled component aging induced by self-heating (SH) under shared electrical stress, (ii) thermal cross-talk (TCT) between neighboring components, and (iii) system lifetimes determined by the temperature profiles of EV trips across the U.S. Incorporating SH and TCT results in a 92% reduction in the predicted active lifetime of a DC-DC converter compared to fixed thermal stress model. Integration of the trip profile further extends model applicability, enabling climate-specific lifetime predictions across different U.S. regions. The proposed framework provides a predictive, stress and temperature-aware reliability tool for robust EV power converter design

title: Victim Circuits for Predicting the ESD Reliability of IO Circuits
authors: P. Sear, S. Huang, Y. Zhou, E. Rosenbaum
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: This work presents electrostatic discharge (ESD) victim circuits designed to fail similarly to real IO circuits. Circuit topology is found to affect the ESD failure level. Electrical measurements are used to identify the device that failed during the ESD testing. Simulation may be used to predict failure levels if carried out with custom ESD compact models and models of the interconnects extracted from electromagnetic simulation.

title: Meta-Learning for GPU-Accelerated Quantum Many-Body Problems
authors: Yun-Hsuan Chen, Jen-Yu Chang, Tsung-Wei Huang, En-Jui Kuo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We explore the industrial and scientific applicability of the VQE-LSTM framework by integrating meta-learning with GPU accelerated quantum simulation using NVIDIA's CUDA-Q (CUDAQ) platform. This work demonstrates how an LSTM-FC meta-initialization module can extend the practical reach of the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) in both chemistry and physics domains. In the chemical regime, the framework predicts ground-state energies of molecular Hamiltonians derived from PySCF, achieving near FCI accuracy while maintaining favorable <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$O\left(N^{2}\right)$</tex> scaling with molecular size. In the physical counterpart, we applied the same model to quantized Simple Harmonic Motion systems (SHM), successfully reproducing its ground and excited states through VQE and Variational Quantum Deflation (VQD) methods. Benchmark results on NVIDIA GPUs reveal significant speedups over CPU-based implementations, validating CUDAQ's capability to handle large-scale variational workloads efficiently. Overall, this study establishes VQE-LSTM as a viable and scalable approach for GPU accelerated quantum simulation, bridging quantum chemistry and condensed-matter physics through a unified, meta-learned initialization strategy.

title: An Adaptive Frequency Charge Pump for High-Side Power Supply in Motor Pre-Driver Chips
authors: Yan Jiao, Mingyu Liu, Yunwu Zhang, Yangyang Lu, Zhikai Meng, Chunwei Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the high-side floating power supply requirements in motor pre-driver chips, charge pump technology has been widely researched and applied. This paper proposes an adaptive frequency charge pump scheme based on voltage difference feedback. Its core innovation lies in the real-time sampling of the voltage difference between the bus voltage and the high-side capacitor voltage, which is proportionally scaled into a continuous control voltage. This linearly modulates the internal oscillator frequency, achieving precise matching between the charging rate and the system's energy demand. Design and simulation based on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.18 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex> BCD process show that, compared to a charge pump with voltage detection functionality, this design reduces the output voltage ripple by 55 % while maintaining the same high-side drive capability and power consumption, and supports 100 % duty cycle operation. Additionally, small-signal analysis confirms the system's inherent stability, and layout considerations combined with the adaptive closed-loop architecture ensure robustness against parasitic effects and process variations. This work provides an innovative high-side power supply solution for high-reliability, high-efficiency motor pre-driver chips.

title: Rate-Fidelity Tradeoffs in All-Photonic and Memory-Equipped Quantum Switches
authors: Panagiotis Promponas, Leonardo Bacciottini, Paul Polakos, Gayane Vardoyan, Don Towsley, Leandros Tassiulas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum entanglement switches are a key building block for early quantum networks, and a central design question is whether near-term devices should use only optical components and photonic qubits or also incorporate quantum memories. We compare two architectures: an all-photonic entanglement generation switch (EGS) that repeatedly attempts Bell-state measurements (BSMs) without storing qubits, and a quantum memory-equipped switch that buffers entanglement and triggers measurements only when heralded connectivity is available (herald-then-swap control). These two designs trade off simple, memoryless operation that avoids decoherence and memory-induced latency against heralding-based control that buffers entanglement to use BSMs more efficiently. We formalize both models under a common hardware abstraction and characterize their achievable rate-fidelity regions, yielding a benchmarking methodology that translates hardware and protocol parameters into network-level performance. Numerical evaluation quantifies the rate-fidelity tradeoffs of both models, identifies operating regions in which each architecture dominates, and shows how hardware and protocol knobs can be tuned to meet application-specific targets.

title: Efficient Time-Aware Partitioning of Quantum Circuits for Distributed Quantum Computing
authors: Raymond P. H. Wu, Chathu Ranaweera, Sutharshan Rajasegarar, Ria Rushin Joseph, Jinho Choi, Seng W. Loke
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: To overcome the physical limitations of scaling monolithic quantum computers, distributed quantum computing (DQC) interconnects multiple smaller-scale quantum processing units (QPUs) to form a quantum network. However, this approach introduces a critical challenge, namely the high cost of quantum communication between remote QPUs incurred by quantum state teleportation and quantum gate teleportation. To minimize this communication overhead, DQC compilers must strategically partition quantum circuits by mapping logical qubits to distributed physical QPUs. Static graph partitioning methods are fundamentally ill-equipped for this task as they ignore execution dynamics and underlying network topology, while metaheuristics require substantial computational runtime. In this work, we propose a heuristic based on beam search to solve the circuit partitioning problem. Our time-aware algorithm incrementally constructs a low-cost sequence of qubit assignments across successive time steps to minimize overall communication overhead. The time and space complexities of the proposed algorithm scale quadratically with the number of qubits and linearly with circuit depth, offering a significant computational speedup over common metaheuristics. We demonstrate that our proposed algorithm consistently achieves significantly lower communication costs than static baselines across varying circuit sizes, depths, and network topologies, providing an efficient compilation tool for near-term distributed quantum hardware.

title: Modeling and Executing Defensive Deceptive Network Policies using the DEVS Formalism
authors: David Haighton, Sylvain P. Leblanc
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Various defensive deceptive network policies have been proposed and mathematically studied in the literature. These policies show promising results as a proactive defense against network intrusions, but none have been sufficiently analyzed for potential deployment on actual networks. While some few have been evaluated through some form of simulation, many have not and virtually none have been analyzed using more realistic mechanisms. As a result, it is difficult to predict if there will be differences between the expected theoretical performance of a policy and its performance in an operational setting. This article introduces work to help network operators gain insight into the potential use of defensive deceptive network policy on their own network. The article presents a generic two-step system called the Defensive Deceptive Network Policy Empirical Evaluation System that helps a network operator gain confidence in a policy’s suitability for use in defending a given network by implementing a defensive deceptive network policy first using discrete event system specifications simulation and second using a testbed. Each of the simulation and testbed implementations produce insights into progressively more realistically representations of an operational network. The system allows a network operator to progressively invest more effort into the investigation of a defensive deceptive network policy which is much less costly than attempting deployment on the operational network under study. The system helps researchers conduct high quality empirical research on defensive deceptive network policies. The article describes how the system is validated through the implementation of a candidate deceptive defensive network policy and its evaluation against a candidate operational network.

title: Hot-Electron Degradation under Switching Accelerated Lifetime Testing (SALT) of 650 V E-Mode GaN HEMTs: A Novel Experimental Approach
authors: M. Dell'Andrea, M. Boito, M. Fregolent, R. Fraccaroli, I. Rossetto, C. De Santi, A. Abbisogni, M. Giuffrida, M. Ruvolo, M. E. Castagna, F. Iucolano, G. Meneghesso, E. Zanoni, M. Meneghini
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: We present a novel experimental setup for Switching Accelerated Lifetime Testing (SALT) based on a boost topology combined with a fast on-state voltage measurement clamp circuit (OVMC). This configuration was validated by investigating the dynamic on-resistance degradation kinetics of on-wafer E-mode GaN HEMTs under hard-switching (HS) operation; an exponential-like increase in R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf> was observed and ascribed to hot-electron trapping at the AlGaN/GaN interface or at the surface. The acceleration factor with voltage under hardswitching operation has been estimated for the first time in GaNbased devices. Finally, Pulsed-Drain Current Transient (P-DCT) measurements allowed to confirm hot-electron trapping as the dominant degradation mechanism: the amplitude of Ron increase extracted from P-DCT Ron transients in the semi-on state exhibited a bell-shaped dependence on gate bias, which is a hallmark signature of hot-electron effects.

title: An Explainable Vision Mamba Framework for Road Crack Classification with Grad-CAM and LIME-Based Superpixel Interpretations
authors: Nymur Rahaman Antor, Nafisa Tasnim, Md Zubair Azam, Sabab Asfaq, Shahriar Siddique Arjon, Tamanna Yasmin
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Cracks that are not detected in a timely manner have a significant impact on traffic safety, infrastructure stability, and maintenance costs. Conventional human-based monitoring is expensive and slow, and existing deep learning solutions are often “black-box” in nature, limiting decision-making transparency. This study presents an interpretable deep learning framework, where the VisionMamba-based classification model is integrated with Grad-CAM and LIME. Specifically, the primary fracture image dataset used in the study was collected by us directly at the field level, which is consistent with the real environment. The proposed model demonstrated excellent performance in the Alligator crack and Edge crack classifications and achieved an overall accuracy of 98.98 %. The prediction process of the model is transparent as the Grad-CAM and LIME outputs clearly show the crack-related region. Overall, this approach provides an effective solution for smart road maintenance and infrastructure management by ensuring accuracy and transparency.

title: Accuracy-Adaptive Speedup of Indirect Optimization Heuristics
authors: Mehreen Khan, Armin Zimmermann
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: This paper addresses a key limitation in simulation-based optimization, where metaheuristics such as simulated annealing (SA) spend excessive time on computationally expensive simulations. We introduce an accuracy-adaptive approach AA-SA that links the simulation part stronger to the SA metaheuristic. Our methodology controls the simulation’s accuracy directly within the SA loop. The search begins with fast simulations to efficiently explore the design space. Simulation accuracy is systematically increased with the convergence process of the SA heuristic. This improves the trade-off between speed and accuracy for simulation-based SA, as simulation cost grows quadratically for a smaller relative error. We further contribute a theoretical model that predicts the speedup achievable by this approach and demonstrate its use with an M/M/1 queue optimization problem. The results confirm that our method achieves significant computational savings while largely preserving the quality of the final solution.

title: Stochastic Modeling of the Physical-Digital Continuum: Assessing Feasibility in Decentralized Environmental Monitoring
authors: Carlos Melo, Jean Araujo
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: The disconnect between physical environmental sensing and digital settlement layers significantly limits the scalability of Voluntary Carbon Markets (VCMs). This gap, often characterized as the “Oracle Problem,” arises from the incompatibility between the probabilistic nature of AI-processed remote sensing data and the deterministic requirements of immutable blockchain ledgers. To mitigate this gap, this paper proposes an Autonomous System-of-Systems (SoS) architecture designed to secure the “Physical-Digital Continuum.” The proposed framework orchestrates the end-to-end flow of environmental assets by integrating high-resolution satellite telemetry, decentralized storage (IPFS), and the Hyperledger Fabric blockchain platform. Unlike traditional feasibility studies, we systematically assess the dynamic behavior of the system using formal modeling techniques. We create a Stochastic Petri Net (SPN) model that clearly captures the concurrency of parallel image processing streams, network synchronization delays, and the stochastic latency of distributed consensus protocols. Stationary analysis reveals that the system is arrival-driven, identifying the blockchain’s payment finality as the primary internal bottleneck limiting scalability. Sensitivity analysis shows that optimizing consensus quorum parameters and settlement latencies can reduce the system’s Mean Response Time (MRT) by $\mathbf{5 0 \%}$ and increase throughput by over $\mathbf{3 0 \%}$. These quantitative results provide a validated reference for implementing scalable, automated Digital Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (dMRV) systems.

title: Enabling Digital Twins for Heterogeneous Battery Systems – An Experience Report
authors: Emil Lundin, Johan Cederbladh, Martin Viker
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Lithium-ion Batteries (LiBs) are one of the more prominent examples of efficient energy storage that can be applied to a wide number of domains. One existing challenge in LiB design is the trade-offs between existing battery cell technology during design towards electrical properties, as no single cell chemistry or cell design is optimal for all operational parameters. A Heterogeneous Battery System (HEBAT) is a means of combining different battery cell technology to enable more flexible architecture in battery systems. A significant tradeoff with a HEBAT towards traditional battery design is added complexity in control and management of the battery. In this context, we propose the use of Digital Twins (DTs) to control and manage HEBATs. This paper describes the design of a HEBAT, and experiences in building custom embedded system software and hardware for management and control to enable DT operation. We demonstrate that HEBATs are a suitable application and use case for DTs, and discuss the current status of our implementation to provide lessons learned.

title: A Compact Physics-Aware Full $I-V$ Model for Nanowire nFET Aging
authors: Michiel Vandemaele, Adrian Chasin, Jacopo Franco, Stanislav Tyaginov, Dieter Claes, Hans Mertens, An De Keersgieter, Geert Hellings, Ben Kaczer
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: We model experimental NanoWire (NW) nFET I-V aging under Bias Temperature Instability (BTI) and Hot-Carrier (HC) stress using our compact physics-aware framework ComphU. We introduce a new simplified and computationally efficient approach to Distribution Function (DF) modeling applicable for short- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$L_{\mathrm{g}}$</tex> FETs and successfully lump the time- 0 variability into a limited parameter set. Our model describes experimental <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta V_{\text {th }}(t)$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta I_{\text {d,on }}(t)$</tex> traces well. Using the calibrated model, we find that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$i$</tex>) the intrinsic time- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0 g_{m, \text { max }}$</tex> is 39 % higher than the extrinsic one, ii) aging outside the calibration region is predicted reasonably, and that the BTI lifetime iii) roughly doubles for every 0.1 V reduction in the supply voltage and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$i v$</tex>) increases up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6.3 \times$</tex> for every 0.1 V increase in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text {th, } 0}$</tex>.

title: Noise Feedback Control and Its Applications to Finite-Time Stabilization of Fuzzy Memristive Reaction-Diffusion Neural Networks
authors: Guanghui Jiang, Leimin Wang, Xiongbo Wan, Guodong Zhang, Song Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: In existing studies on neural networks (NNs) stabilization, stochastic disturbances are typically regarded as negative factors. In contrast, this paper systematically explores the positive role of stochastic disturbances in the stabilization of NNs and proposes a novel finite-time noise feedback control method. By rationally utilizing stochastic disturbances, the originally unstable fuzzy memristive NNs with reaction-diffusion components achieve finite-time stochastic stabilization. Meanwhile, some less conservative finite-time stabilization criteria are proposed, eliminating the requirement in classical criteria that Lyapunov function’s differential operator must be strictly negative. The novel criteria not only extend the application scope of existing stochastic stabilization from exponential stabilization to finite-time case, but also elaborately explore the relationship between noise intensity and the convergence speed of the system. Finally, the effectiveness of derived results is verified through simulation.

title: Influence of μm-level profile slopes on vacuum DC flashover: a model guided design optimization
authors: Yuhang Huang, Sile Chen, Chao Wang, Yuying Wang, Zhaoquan Chen, Kehan Cao, Baipeng Song, Xiong Yang, Guanjun Zhang, Guangyu Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Surface microstructures (<1 mm) have important influences on the surface insulation strength of insulating materials, which are typically quantified by surface roughness. However, conventional roughness parameters, such as the arithmetic mean height of profile (Ra), cannot distinguish complex microstructures, potentially resulting in different insulation performances of materials with the same roughness. This study proposes a parameter, the sum of unit-length profile slopes (denoted as Rk), which is suitable for characterizing the surface roughness, especially the surface with a structure of regular grooves. Alumina ceramic samples with the same Ra and different Rk are obtained by laser surface grooving, and the effect of Rk on surface insulation performance is studied systematically. It is found that as Rk increased, the surface conductivity showed an upward trend, the surface potential decay of the samples accelerated, the secondary electron yield decreased, and the vacuum flashover voltage increased. Properly adjusting Rk can effectively enhance the strength of the surface insulation of alumina ceramic. The combination of Ra and Rk can serve as a useful tool for the design of surface structures on insulating materials.

title: Design and Analysis of a Miniaturized SIW Antenna for Tactical UAV Communication Systems
authors: Sairam Baskaran, Siva Bala Murugan.B, L.Magthelin Therase, Poonguzhali. S, Vinoth M, Mukesh C.K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: This paper presents a new class of integrated hexagonal structured substrate Integrated Waveguide (SIW) filter and microstrip patch antenna. UHF wavelengths are comparatively long, and antennas intended for this band must therefore utilize correspondingly larger resonant dimensions. This creates a major challenge when integrating antennas into compact airborne platforms such as Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). The SIW technology was used to reduce the entire wave to a quarter wave. This robust antenna was designed for UAV applications and is capable of long-distance data transfer in the UHF band. Operating at 1.0765 GHz, the developed antenna is a single port microstrip patch antenna. The dielectric substrate that is used is FR-4. It provides sufficient efficiency and reliable performance because of its low cost, robust mechanical design, and low dielectric loss at UHF frequencies. The insert feed provides improved impedance matching and the antenna size decreases from full-wave to half-wave and quarter-wave structures to maintain stable UHF radiation performance and bandwidth. The simulation of this task was performed using Ansys HFSS software while operating within the UHF frequency range of 0.3 to 3 GHz and using a reduced size model that allows for installation.

title: Boosting Large Vision-Language Models with Hierarchical Semantic Fusion for Remote Sensing Visual Question Answering
authors: Fan Liu, Jian Huo, Dongmei Wang, Yihong Wang, Yi Xu, Wenwen Dai, Yuexuan An
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Remote Sensing Visual Question Answering aims to answer natural language queries by jointly modeling visual content and semantic meaning in remote sensing imagery. Large Vision Language Models offer strong visual understanding and reasoning capabilities and provide a promising foundation for VQA tasks. However, existing LVLMs are not well adapted to remote sensing scenes, as deep layer focused visual representations tend to overlook low and mid level structural cues required for RS specific reasoning. To address this limitation, we propose the Hierarchical Semantic Fusion Network, HSF-Net, a remote-sensing-oriented enhancement framework built upon LLaVA-v1.5 to balance detail perception and semantic reasoning in RS-VQA. HSF-Net integrates three complementary modules: 1) Frequency-Selective Unsharp Masking amplifies high-frequency structural details in RS imagery; 2) Hierarchical Cross-Level Semantic Fusion injects multi-scale shallow and mid-level cues into deep semantic features while preserving language alignment; 3) Self-Attention-based Semantic Reweighting refines internal semantic correlations to strengthen contextual understanding. Extensive experimental evaluations on three benchmark RS-VQA datasets, compared with lightweight specialized models and superior LVLMs, demonstrate HSF-Net’s state-of-the-art performance across all tasks. Our code is available at: https://github.com/HUOUO7/HSF-Net.

title: Effect of Seawater Corrosion on Aging Performance of Cross-linked Polyethylene Cable Insulation
authors: Yi Zhang, Yuyang Wang, Dongdong Zhang, Wenbo Zhang, Zaijun Wu, Minqiang Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Owing to the complex operation environment in the ocean, the external structure of submarine XLPE cable is vulnerable to damage, enabling seawater infiltration and subsequent irreversible degradation of the cable's insulation material. This study primarily seeks to clarify the effect of seawater corrosion on the aging performance of XLPE cable insulation. To this end, accelerated seawater corrosion samples are systematically examined through several aging evaluation methods, including SEM, FTIR, DSC, and AC electrical breakdown tests. Further, microscopic structural changes within the XLPE cable insulation during seawater corrosion are analyzed through molecular simulations. Ultimately, based on the above research results, the mechanism by which seawater corrosion contributes to the deterioration of cable insulation is elucidated and then a preliminary study on material modification is conducted. Test results indicate that there is a non-monotonic evolution of insulation performance with exposure time, characterized by an initial improvement followed by progressive deterioration. Increasing seawater concentration accelerates degradation but exhibits a clear saturation effect beyond a threshold. Furthermore, nanofiller incorporation, particularly Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>, significantly enhances insulation performance by reducing mass loss and suppressing corrosive species diffusion, which can provide some help in future research to improve the long-term durability of XLPE cable in the marine condition.

title: In-Situ Hardware Error Detection Using Specification-Derived Petri Net Models and Behavior-Derived State Sequences
authors: Tomonari Tanaka, Takumi Uezono, Kohei Suenaga, Masanori Hashimoto
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: In hardware accelerators used in data centers and safety-critical applications, soft errors and resultant silent data corruption significantly compromise reliability, particularly when upsets occur in control-flow operations, leading to severe failures. To address this issue, we introduce two methods for monitoring control flows: a method using specification-derived Petri nets and a method using behavior-derived state transitions, targeting applications where the control flow is independent of the datapath, with the assumption of manageable operation patterns. We validated our method across four designs: convolutional layer operation, Gaussian blur, AES encryption, and a router in Network-on-Chip. Our fault injection campaign targeting the control registers and primary control inputs demonstrated high error detection rates in both datapath and control logic. Synthesis results show that a maximum detection rate is achieved with a few to around 10% area overhead in most cases. The proposed detectors quickly detect 48% to 100% of failures resulting from upsets in internal control registers and perturbations in primary control inputs. The two proposed methods were compared in terms of area overhead and error detection rate. By selectively applying these two methods, a wide range of area constraints can be accommodated, enabling practical implementation and effectively enhancing error detection capabilities.

title: ArtNet: Hierarchical Clustering-Based Artificial Netlist Generator for ML and DTCO Applications
authors: Andrew B. Kahng, Seokhyeong Kang, Seonghyeon Park, Dooseok Yoon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: In advanced nodes, optimization of power, performance and area (PPA) has become highly complex and challenging. Machine learning (ML) and design-technology co-optimization (DTCO) provide promising mitigations, but face limitations due to a lack of diverse training data as well as long design flow turnaround times (TAT). We propose ArtNet, a novel artificial netlist generator designed to tackle these issues. Unlike previous methods, ArtNet replicates key topological characteristics, enhancing ML model generalization and supporting broader design space exploration for DTCO. By producing realistic artificial datasets that more closely match given target parameters, ArtNet enables more efficient PPA optimization and exploration of flows and design enablements. In the context of CNN-based DRV prediction, ArtNet’s data augmentation improves F1 score by 0.16 compared to using only the original (real) dataset. In the DTCO context, ArtNet-generated mini-brains achieve a PPA match up to 97.94%, demonstrating close alignment with design metrics of targeted full-scale block designs.

title: Share Your Preprint Research with the World!

publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine

title: Decoupled Per-Phase Control of a Four-Leg Grid-Feeding Inverter for Voltage Unbalance Mitigation in Low-Voltage Networks
authors: Iñaki Aizpuru, Jose Antonio Cortajarena, Estefania Planas, Iñigo Kortabarria, Iker Aretxabaleta
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The increasing integration of distributed energy resources and single-phase loads in low-voltage distribution networks introduces significant voltage unbalance, degrading power quality and compromising supply reliability. Existing four-leg inverter solutions typically rely on grid-forming operation or sequence-component decomposition, limiting their applicability in grid-connected scenarios with parallel utility supply. This paper proposes a decoupled per-phase current control strategy for a four-leg inverter operating exclusively in grid-feeding mode, which constitutes the main scientific contribution of this work. The method assigns an independent proportional-resonant controller to each phase, enabling per-phase active power regulation and bidirectional current flowwithout sequence-component transformation, ensuring stable parallel operation with the utility network. A DC bus provides the necessary voltage stiffness for current regulation. Experimental validation under diverse unbalanced operating conditions demonstrates a reduction of the voltage unbalance ratio to zero, with total harmonic distortion of injected currents remaining below 15% in all tested cases. These results confirm the suitability of the proposed framework for low-voltage networks with high penetration of distributed generation.

title: Expanded Deep Embedding Clustering With Adversarial Learning and Adaptive Graph Constraint_supp1-3688565.pdf
authors: Jin Zhou
publish: N/A
abstract: The autoencoder (AE) is an efficient feature extraction tool that learns latent representations from raw data by minimizing the reconstruction loss. Building upon the AE architecture, deep clustering models are designed to jointly optimize the deep neural network and perform unsupervised clustering. However, existing methods directly impose the clustering objective on the latent features produced by the AE network, thereby neglecting the potential conflict between data clustering and data representation. Specifically, data clustering aims to enhance data aggregation, whereas data representation focuses on ensuring that latent features faithfully reflect the manifold structure of the raw data. To address this issue, this article proposes an innovative expanded deep embedding clustering (E-DEC) model, in which the AE network is employed to seek better latent representations, and a novel residual expansion module (REM) is integrated to construct an expanded feature space that better serves clustering tasks. Furthermore, adversarial learning between the soft cluster assignments and a prior one-hot distribution is adopted in lieu of the conventional Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence, so as to enhance the discrimination of different clusters and avoid the degeneracy problem. Finally, an entropy regularization technique is incorporated to adaptively refine the affinity graph throughout the clustering process, thereby reducing the sensitivity of clustering performance to the initial affinity graph. Extensive experiments on real-world benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed model over state-of-the-art deep clustering methods.

title: Physics-Aware Deep Reinforcement Learning for Energy and Aging Management in Electrified Powertrains
authors: Md Ragib Rownak, Wafeeq Jaleel, Athar Hanif, Muhammad Qaisar Fahim, Dat D Le, Hamza Anwar, Matthew Nelson, Qadeer Ahmed
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: This study develops an aging-aware deep reinforcement learning framework for heavy-duty electrified powertrains, optimizing fuel consumption jointly with three physics-based degradation dynamics: battery calendar and cycling aging and generator thermal aging. The discrete–continuous actions are addressed using Physics-Aware Soft Actor–Critic (PA-SAC) agents, and training stability is enhanced by a Dynamic Programming (DP)-based warm start combining behavior cloning, expert replay-buffer seeding, and critic warmup. The framework is applied to a range-extender series-hybrid architecture, with agents trained on the Heavy Heavy-Duty Diesel Truck (HHDDT) cycle and evaluated on the training cycle and two unseen test cycles under a terminal state-of-charge (SOC) constraint of [0.5480, 0.5520]. Benchmarked across five seeds per case against DP optima, Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3), and Proximal Policy Optimization with Lagrangian constraints (PPO-L), PA-SAC attains the tightest DP gap on the training cycle and lowest seed-to-seed variability. Aging objectives reshape the control strategy, reducing battery capacity degradation due to cycling aging by up to 91% relative to the fuel-only baseline and redistributing load between the battery and engine-generator subsystems under the aging-aware objective. The trained policy is deployed on an embedded controller target in a controller hardware-in-the-loop testbench, reproducing the training-time control behavior across twelve runs.

title: Fault Diagnosis and Fault Tolerance Method for Position Sensor in DSEG Based on Line Voltage and Position Signal
authors: Yixi Chen, Bo Zhou, Yi Lu, Yijun Zhang, Jiadan Wei
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: The stable operation of controlled rectifier system in doubly salient electromagnetic generator (DSEG) is highly dependent on the reliability of position sensor. The fault of position sensor can cause commutation failure, leading to degraded power generation performance and even system instability. To address this issue, this article proposes a fault diagnosis and fault tolerance method for position sensor based on line voltage and position signal. The method accurately identifies rotor sector and estimates rotor position in real time by monitoring line voltage. By analyzing identified sector state, estimated rotor position, and position signal, fault diagnosis is performed for single, two, or three position sensors. For single or two position sensor faults, a fault tolerance method is studied based on position signal reconstruction, while for three position sensor faults, a position sensorless tolerance method is presented by utilizing line voltage. The proposed method is applicable under conditions of varying rotor speed and load, and the maximum time of error commutation is one thirty-sixth of an electrical angle period. Finally, the effectiveness of proposed method is validated on a 18/12 pole DSEG experimental platform equipped with three hall position sensors.

title: Wind Turbine Enhanced Power Recovery Through Overrating of In-Service Permanent Magnet Generators
authors: Aras Ghafoor, Judith Apsley, Siniša Djurović
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: With increasing global energy demand, extracting higher output power from in-service wind turbines is a cost-effective option for wind farm operators. This paper presents a novel strategy for Enhanced Power Recovery through Overrating (EPRO) of a permanent magnet synchronous generator (PMSG), which increases generator currents above nominal rating in the turbine’s constant power operating region. Innovative temperature sensing in the stator windings protects against overheating and field weakening control is integrated with the turbine pitch control to obtain higher current, whilst remaining within the original inverter voltage limit. Operation in the maximum power point tracking region is unchanged. A custom integrated electro-thermal model of the wind generator is used at the design stage to identify hotspots and ensure magnet and winding temperatures are not exceeded. A grid-connected, kW-scale test rig with built-in Fibre Bragg temperature sensors provides experimental validation. The EPRO strategy is compared for different dynamic wind speed profiles, with good agreement between simulation and experiment. Laboratory results demonstrate a sustained increase in generated power of up to 26% above conventional operation, whilst remaining 30°C below the insulation temperature constraint. At MW-scale, the power increase would depend on the PMSG thermal design, the required upgrade in grid interface hardware, and availability of the wind resource.

title: Through a Glass Darkly [Microwave Surfing]
authors: Rajeev Bansal
publish: IEEE Microwave Magazine
