Therefore, this paper proposes a self-healing method for cellulose insulating paper based on isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI) microcapsules. First, a three-dimensional crack model of cellulose and its cured polyurethane product was constructed using molecular dynamics simulations to simulate and analyze the process of polyurethane filling the microcracks. Then, the diffusion behavior of the crack model was investigated under three temperature conditions (334 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{K}, 364 \mathrm{K}$</tex>, and 374 K). Finally, the mechanical property recovery after crack repair was evaluated. The results show that the selfhealing process can be divided into two stages: morphological recovery and performance recovery. At 374 K, the repair system exhibits the highest diffusion rate and the most significant recovery of mechanical properties, indicating the best repair effect. The proposed method intuitively demonstrates the entire performance recovery process of transformers under operating temperatures and endows cellulose insulating paper with the ability to achieve self-healing without the need for external conditions.

title: Explainable Multitask Graph Network for Blood Glucose Forecasting and Hypoglycemia Risk Classification
authors: Muhammad Abdullah Sarwar, Sarmad Maqsood, Ali Amini, Irfan Abbas, Egle Belousoviene, Rytis Maskeliūnas
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: To effectively manage Type 1 Diabetes (T1D), it is helpful to accurately forecast glucose levels and detect hypoglycemia early, as timely intervention can prevent serious complications associated with hypoglycemia. Contemporary predictive models frequently treat both tasks as isolated processes and often exhibit limited interpretability, thus reducing their practical utility in clinical settings. This study introduces an explainable multitask graph neural network (MT-GNN) designed to simultaneously execute prediction and classification tasks. The framework is based on a physiology-inspired graph structure to depict multimodal signals, including continuous glucose monitoring (CGM), insulin, carbohydrate intake, and activity, within their physiological context, thereby elucidating significant interdependencies. The experimental evaluation utilizing the BrisT1D dataset indicates superior multitask performance, generating a coefficient of determination ($R^{2}$) of 0.95 for glucose prediction and an accuracy of $98.88 \%$ for the detection of the risk of hypoglycemia. MT-GNN has achieved a lower prediction error and significant discriminative ability compared to singletask graph neural networks and classical baseline models. The explanation tools were developed, which help with transparency, trust, and ease of use. Thus, the proposed model is a significant step towards safer, more proactive, and more effective diabetes treatment management, enhancing personalized care and providing a foundation for future clinical implementation.

title: Barrier Integrity Evaluation Methods in Copper Interconnects
authors: Kayla Sanders, Liang-Shan Chen, Ying-Hong Zhao, Ki-Don Lee, Phyo Thuzar, Pingan Fang, Manisha Sharma, Timothy Davis, Ju-Kwang Kim, Dung Dau, Mukyeng Jung
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: The electromigration (EM) immortality, exhibiting no sign of failure even after a prolonged duration of stress, can be attributed to the continuous supply of Cu from a wide underlying feeder through the via with poor barrier coverage. This type of barrier defect can artificially inflate the estimated EM lifetime, leading to a misleadingly optimistic reliability risk assessment in the lab. EM risk can also emerge in actual products due to the stringent design and aggressive scaling in advanced interconnects, especially with short-length EM rules. For EM risk mitigation, it is essential to have effective methods to reveal such barrier defects. This paper presents two PFA techniques for barrier integrity evaluation. The chemical etch method is very simple yet effective, and can be readily performed in general PFA labs. The STEM tomography requires an advanced TEM but can pinpoint the defect location.

title: DNACrypt: A Secure DNA and Steganography Based Post-Quantum Framework with Content-Based Keying
authors: Md Farhan Shahriyar, Md. Abdulla Al Mamun
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Post-quantum cyber threats with their increasing complexity and pervasiveness demand security solutions that go beyond conventional, isolated defenses. Cryptography protects the content of a message but reveals that secret communication is taking place. Steganography hides the existence of communication but does not protect the content itself. This paper proposes a novel hybrid security model that synergistically integrates bio-inspired DNA cryptography, AES encryption and Least Significant Bit (LSB) steganography. The framework introduces a deterministic key generation mechanism derived from the cover image's intrinsic content eliminating key exchange overhead and enhancing security. The secret message undergoes a dual-phase transformation: first, it is encrypted using a DNA-based XOR cipher conditioned by the content-based key and second, the resulting DNA sequence is further encrypted using AES in CBC mode. The final ciphertext is covertly embedded into the cover image using LSB substitution. Empirical evaluation demonstrates the model's efficacy achieving high visual imperceptibility (PSNR <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>50 \text{dB}$</tex>, SSIM 1.00) and low Bit Error Rate (BER 0) confirming its resilience against detection and cryptanalysis.

title: A Deep Learning Based Ensemble Approach for Digital Image Forgery Detection
authors: Anik Deb Nath, Sourav Adhikary
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of digital media has raised significant concerns regarding the authenticity of images shared across online platforms. Image forgery, which involves tampering with visual content, can result in misinformation, legal disputes, and security threats. In this paper, we propose a deep learning–based system for image forgery detection that not only classifies images as authentic or tampered but also localizes the manipulated regions. The proposed framework integrates multiple CNN architectures trained on benchmark datasets, including CASIA and MICC, and employs a fusion strategy based on soft voting to enhance robustness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves reliable detection performance across various manipulation types, such as copy–move and splicing. Comparative analysis with existing approaches shows competitive accuracy while maintaining strong generalization to unseen forgeries. Overall, the proposed framework offers a scalable and effective solution for combating digital image tampering, making it highly applicable to real-world forensic and security contexts.

title: Solving Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem with Quantum Alternating Operator Ansatz and Column Generation
authors: Wei-Hao Huang, Hiromichi Matsuyama, Yu Yamashiro
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: This study proposes a hybrid quantum-classical approach to solving the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) by integrating the Column Generation (CG) method with the Quantum Alternating Operator Ansatz (QAOAnsatz). The CG method divides the CVRP into the reduced master problem, which finds the best combination of the routes under the route set, and one or more subproblems, which generate the routes that would be beneficial to add to the route set. This method is iteratively refined by adding new routes identified via subproblems and continues until no improving route can be found. We leverage the QAOAnsatz to solve the subproblems. Our algorithm restricts the search space by designing the QAOAnsatz mixer Hamiltonian to enforce one-hot constraints. Moreover, to handle capacity constraints in QAOAnsatz, we employ an Augmented Lagrangian-inspired method that obviates the need for additional slack variables, reducing the required number of qubits. Experimental results on small-scale CVRP instances (up to 6 customers) show that QAOAnsatz converges more quickly to optimal routes than the standard QAOA approach, demonstrating the potential of this hybrid framework in tackling realworld logistical optimization problems on near-term quantum hardware.

title: Burst Mode Triangular Pulse CV System for Reliability Analysis of Two-Terminal Multilevel Memory Devices
authors: Himanshu Marothya, Sandip Mondal
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: Reliable characterization of two-terminal multilevel charge-trapping memory devices requires fast readout, large-signal voltage sweeps, and integrated high-voltage program/erase capability. In this work, a burst-mode triangular capacitance-voltage (BMTCV) measurement system is presented for large-signal characterization of such devices. The system captures a complete CV curve within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \mu$</tex> s using a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 12 \mathrm{V}$</tex> triangular ramp and incorporates a high-voltage stress module capable of applying program and erase pulses up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm \mathbf{3 0 ~ V}$</tex>. Unlike conventional small-signal CV techniques, the proposed approach enables ultrafast readout immediately after electrical stress, minimizing read-induced disturbance. The BMTCV system is applied to an eight-level CdTe quantum-dot-based two-terminal memory device. Clear separation of all programmed states is achieved, and systematic retention and endurance measurements are performed using the same measurement platform. The results demonstrate that the BMTCV system provides a practical and reliable solution for large-signal, low-disturb characterization of multilevel charge-trapping memories under realistic operating conditions.

title: Topology-Hiding Connectivity-Assurance for QKD Inter-Networking
authors: Margherita Cozzolino, Stephan Krenn, Thomas Lorünser
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: While QKD ensures information-theoretic security at the link level, real-world deployments depend on trusted repeaters, creating potential vulnerabilities. In this paper, we thus introduce a topology-hiding connectivity assurance protocol to enhance trust in quantum key distribution (QKD) network infrastructures. Our protocol allows network providers to jointly prove the existence of a secure connection between endpoints without revealing internal topology details. By extending graph-signature techniques to support multi-graphs and hidden endpoints, we enable zero-knowledge proofs of connectivity that ensure both soundness and topology hiding. We further discuss how our approach can certify, e.g., multiple disjoint paths, supporting multi-path QKD scenarios. This work bridges cryptographic assurance methods with the operational requirements of QKD networks, promoting verifiable and privacy-preserving inter-network connectivity.

title: Dependency-Aware Circuit Scheduling for Multi-Core Quantum Systems to Minimize Makespan
authors: Rajeswari Suance P S, Ruchika Gupta, Maurizio Palesi, John Jose
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Multi-core quantum computing architectures have emerged as a promising solution to the qubit scalability limitations of monolithic NISQ devices. Quantum algorithms are expressed as quantum circuits composed of single- and twoqubit gates. However, circuit scheduling in multi-core quantum systems remains largely unexplored. Reducing overall execution time (makespan), increasing core utilization, and hiding communication latency behind computation depends on effective scheduling. In this paper, we first introduce a layered scheduling approach as a baseline where quantum gates within the same layer are executed in parallel, while layers themselves are executed sequentially. We then propose a greedy scheduling strategy which schedules each gate as soon as all its dependencies and required resources are available. This allows finegrained parallelism across cores. Our evaluation shows that on real benchmarks, greedy scheduling achieves an average 40% reduction in makespan and improvement in core utilization. The results suggest that the use of intelligent circuit scheduling to exploit parallelism can greatly enhance the speed of circuit execution in multi-core quantum architectures.

title: End-to-End QKD using LEO Satellite Networks
authors: Sumit Chaudhary, Baqir Kazmi, Janis Nötzel
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We propose a satellite-based Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) network that enables global-scale, end-to-end secure key exchange without relying on trusted intermediate nodes. The network is formed by a ring constellation of satellites that maintain persistent inter-satellite connectivity and support two configurations: a polar Type-I constellation providing global coverage, and an equatorial Type-II constellation offering continuous, terrestrial-like operation. End-to-end secrecy is achieved through the use of Twin-field Quantum Key Distribution (TFQKD) and a redundant XOR-based key-forwarding protocol, in which each forwarding step incorporates independently generated QKD keys from ground–satellite and inter-satellite links. As a result, the final secret key is never exposed to any intermediate satellite, eliminating the single-point vulnerabilities inherent in trusted-node networks. Scaling the network offers two benefits: improved security and higher key rates. Increasing the constellation size enhances security by forcing an adversary to compromise a larger number of nodes to break the protocol, while simultaneously improving link availability and key throughput. Using realistic uplink and Inter-Satellite Link (ISL) models, we compute finite-size secret-key lengths based on the Sending-or-not-sending (SNS)–TF-QKD protocol. Our results show that the achievable key rates scale favourably with constellation size, with Type-II constellations reaching operational continuity and generating multi-gigabit secret keys per day, demonstrating a practical route toward secure global quantum communication.

title: Leveraging Ensemble Learning Models to Forecast Agricultural Yield in Bangladesh Under Climate Impact
authors: Shovan Samanta Turzo, Shafin Ibnul Mohasin, Mahamudul Hasan Sourov, Md Jahidul Islam Moon, Taifur Rahman, Md Muhasin Ali
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The precise forecasting of agricultural productivity under climate change is essential for food security where many areas are sensitive. In this work, a multiclass classification problem is shown for categorizing crop yield as Low, Medium and High using climate and environment variables of Bangladesh. We apply the proposed method to a dataset with features such as rainfall, average temperature, drought severity, flood impact, cyclone frequency and air quality comparing the performance of SAINT (self-attention for tabular data), NODE (Neural Oblivious Decision Ensembles) and Support Vector Machine (SVM). Furthermore, we also consider a stacked ensemble. Results indicate that 99% accuracy is achieved by the ensemble model, which is superior to three single models: SAINT (95.33%), NODE (96.83%) and SVM (95.50%). The feature importance analysis indicates that rainfall and average temperature are the most influential features for predicting agricultural yield. This study indicates that hybrid models are effective in monitoring the climate impacts on agriculture, which can provide decision-making supports for stakeholders, policy makers, and agronomists.

title: Optimizing Resource Allocation in a Distributed Quantum Computing Cloud: a Game-Theoretic Approach
authors: Bernard Ousmane Sane, Michal Hajdušek, Rodney Van Meter
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum cloud computing will play a vital role in achieving quantum supremacy by providing flexible allocation of resources for applications that demand extensive computational power. Such a platform allows various clients to run their quantum jobs (quantum circuits) without managing the quantum hardware and to pay based on resource usage. Therefore, defining optimal quantum resource allocation is essential to prevent overcharging clients and to help quantum cloud providers maximize resource utilization. Our approach involves examining the problem from a game theory perspective. We introduce a quantum circuit partitioning resource allocation game model (QC-PRAGM) designed to minimize client costs and inter-node communication. This is achieved by solving a convex optimization problem that finds the system's optimal cost while maximizing the number of local gates within a partition through the selection of the best qubit combinations. We demonstrate analytically that clients are charged fairly. Additionally, our simulations show that our solutions outperform traditional methods.

title: Quantum Approximate Optimization for Targeted Weight Removal in Large Language Models
authors: Don Roosan, Rubayat Khan, Avik Mahata, Mazharul Karim
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Large language models enable powerful text understanding and generation but can inadvertently memorize sensitive or disallowed information. We present a practical pipeline for targeted removal of such knowledge by operating directly at the parameter level. The approach combines classical attribution to nominate influential weights with quantum-assisted refinement and selection. Quantum Gradient Descent refines gradient signals to prioritize parameters most responsible for the hazardous snippet, and the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm frames final pruning as a discrete subset selection problem that balances thorough unlearning with preservation of general capability. In experiments on a model intentionally imbued with a hazardous snippet, the hybrid method sharply reduced adversarial recovery of the removed content while maintaining near-baseline fluency after brief clean fine-tuning. The pipeline also showed greater resistance to re-learning the snippet under subsequent exposure, suggesting deeper disruption of the underlying representation. These results indicate that quantum-assisted pruning offers a viable path for robust, auditable unlearning in safety- and privacy-critical deployments of large language models.

title:
Study of Heavy-Ion-Induced Degradation in Si
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    N
                    <sub>4</sub>
                    MIM Capacitors for GaN Power Amplifiers
authors: Samrat Roy Chowdhury, Isabella Rossetto, Carlo De Santi, Manuel Fregolent, Andrea. Carlotto, Fabiana Rampazzo, Francesco De Pieri, Alessio Pantellini, Marco Natali, Luca Latessa, Gaudenzio Meneghesso, Matteo Meneghini, Enrico Zanoni
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: This work discusses the catastrophic failure in thick SiN metal-insulator-metal (MIM) capacitors aimed at GaN power amplifiers and submitted to heavy-ion irradiation. Results show that: (i) breakdown (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BD</inf>) occurs at lower voltage under irradiation; (ii) <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{B D}$</tex> and corresponding dispersion depend on the linear energy transfer (LET) of the ion species used; (iii) trend with LET does not depend on MIM area; (iv) thick SiN layers show cumulative degradation when tested with constant bias stress under irradiation; (v) degradation is identified by two signatures: a recoverable change in leakage current; a non-recoverable variation of charge trapping identified by the hysteresis in I-V curves which, after irradiation, can be modulated by both ultraviolet (UV) light and by the applied bias.

title: Predicting Error Types and Timing in Quay Crane Operations with eXtreme Gradient Boosting
authors: Robert Klar, Vangelis Angelakis
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Efficient port operations depend on the disruptionfree operation of quay cranes (QCs), which transfer containers between vessels and internal trucks. As global container throughput rises, QCs face increased pressure, resulting in accelerated wear and tear. This can lead to QC downtime, which could interrupt the entire chain of port operations. Therefore, timely identification and prediction of critical errors is essential to enable timely maintenance to lower the risk of downtime. This study utilizes two years of QC monitoring data, enriched with weather conditions and terminal operational context, alongside twenty critical error events identified by the terminal operator. The goal is to predict the occurrence and timing of these critical errors through a three-stage machine learning model. The first stage predicts the type of the next critical event based on historical error patterns, warnings, and contextual data. The second stage estimates a time window in which the event will occur. The third stage refines timing predictions when more than one hour remains. The first two stages are formulated as multiclass classification problems, and the third as a regression task. All stages utilize eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost). SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) are used to identify influential features. Results show that the model predicts the next critical error type with $83 \%$ accuracy and its immediacy with $71 \%$ accuracy. However, approximating the timing of events anticipated to occur beyond one hour remains challenging. These findings support proactive maintenance planning and operational adjustments, helping port operators mitigate disruptions and enhance QC reliability.

title: A Physics-Based Modeling Framework for Long-Term Retention Loss in 3D Nand Arrays
authors: Anuj Kumar, Tarun Samadder, Karansingh Thakor, Nikhil Rangarajan, Souvik Mahapatra, Shyam Raghunathan, Yingda Dong
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: A physics-based Activated Barrier Double Well Thermionic (ABDWT) framework is validated on Long-Term retention loss in 176-, 232-, and 276- layer 3D TLC NAND Flash arrays under different program levels, patterns, number of Program/Erase cycles and bake temperatures. Consistent segmentation of retention loss into contributing charge loss components is shown. This is the first framework applicable across multiple 3D-NAND nodes with consistent parameters, surpassing empirical methods and enabling broader applicability, versatility and robustness in retention loss modeling for 3D NAND.

title: Proposal of AI-Based Magnetic Field Estimation Methods
authors: Haruna Takaki, Ryota Kinjo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: We propose projection-to-field network (PF-Net), a deep-learning method for single-shot estimation of the three-dimensional vector magnetic field <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${{{\bm{B}}}_{\bm{x}}},{{{\bm{B}}}_{\bm{y}}},{{{\bm{B}}}_{\bm{z}}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> on an observation plane from a single magneto-optical polarization rotation angle map <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\bm{\theta }}( {{\bm{x}},{\bm{y}}} )$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Although estimating three field components from a single scalar observation is underdetermined at the pixel level, PF-Net stabilizes this inverse problem by exploiting spatial context inherent in convolutional architectures, which provides implicit regularization in the form of smoothness and noise suppression. The model was trained and evaluated using 10,000 synthetic cases generated with the magnetic simulator MagTense. The performance of the model was compared against several baselines: a conventional analytical method, pixel-wise linear regression, and two methods based on regularized least squares (RLS)—namely, a variational RLS-based joint vector-field reconstruction method (JVVR) and an RLS-based two-stage potential-field reconstruction method (TPR). Under the fixed-test single-channel condition, PF-Net achieved MAE = 0.042 mT, rMAE = 0.458 ± 0.039%, and angle error = 0.420 ± 0.023 °, substantially outperforming all baselines. We further evaluated its robustness to nonlinear response, sensor-parameter variations, measurement-noise out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs, observation-model mismatch, and distribution shift. In particular, explicit regularization via in-plane divergence suppression (Div2D) contributed to improved robustness under measurement-degradation conditions. These results demonstrate that PF-Net is a promising data-driven framework for single-shot vector magnetic-field estimation in magneto-optical imaging.

title: RayDETR: Ray-based Geometric Consistency for UAV Multi-View 3D Object Detection
authors: Mingkui Feng, Hancheng Yu, Xiaoyu Dang, Zhongchen Li
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have become essential platforms for 3D object detection. In contrast to DETR-style 2D detectors, the Euclidean distance between 3D predictions and the ground truth does not serve as an accurate proxy for sampling error due to complex projection geometry. To overcome this limitation, we propose the perpendicular distance from a predicted point to the ray connecting the camera optical center and the ground-truth, which serves as a precise geometric measure of sampling difficulty. Although this ray distance provides an ideal sampling reference, the isotropic circularity resulting from equal ray distances constrains the approach's ability to capture object shape and orientation. We therefore introduce an anisotropy-aware elliptical mapping that reformulates the circular projection into an ellipse aligned with the object's geometry. By integrating the ellipse-corrected ray distances into both the bipartite matching cost and the regression loss, we develop RayDETR-a geometry-consistent, multi-view 3D detection framework. Experiments on the UAV3D dataset demonstrate that RayDETR achieves state-of-the-art performance without compromising inference speed.

title: AEFormer: A Hybrid Conv1D-Transformer Model for Concrete Damage Classification via Acoustic Emission Signals
authors: Aman Kumar Gaur, Nipun Bansal, Yashasvi Bansal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Trends in Information Technology (ICITIIT)
abstract: Acoustic Emission (AE) signals are an attractive, non-destructive testing technique for detecting damage in concrete structures; however, computational resource limitations limit the potential of applying deep learning-based methods for instant structural health monitoring (SHM) across all types of micro-structural health monitoring systems, with both high levels of accuracy and efficiency. The paper proposed AEFormer, a hybrid lightweight network comprising a 1D convolutional layer for local feature extraction, followed by a Transformer encoder that learns global patterns across the sequence of AE signals. With fewer than 28,000 trainable parameters, AEFormer achieved 99.82 % test accuracy, 0.0052 test loss, and per-class F1 scores >0.998, and significantly outperformed state-of-theart lightweight CNN and Tiny ANN baseline models. Therefore, these results indicate that AEFormer is a viable candidate for balancing accuracy and efficiency in real-time SHM, particularly on embedded platforms.

title: Load Flow Analysis of an Islanded Microgrid Using Modified Newton-Raphson Method
authors: Supraja Kethari, Srihitha Maddiboyina, Nandini Nadiminti, Archana Kanakadurga, Chandini Moode, Shaik Rafi Kiran
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS)
abstract: The rapid growth of distributed energy resources and renewable generation has led to increased interest in microgrids as a reliable and flexible solution for modern power systems. When operating in islanded mode, microgrids are isolated from the main utility grid and must independently regulate voltage and frequency using local distributed generators (DGs). In such conditions, the absence of a slack bus and the presence of frequency variations invalidate the assumptions of conventional load flow techniques. This paper presents a modified Newton-Raphson method for islanded microgrids with droop-controlled DGs. In this method, the system frequency is treated as a state variable, and included voltage and frequency-dependent load models represent real-time operating conditions. A six-bus islanded microgrid is modelled and simulated in MATLAB to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The performance of the modified Newton-Raphson method is compared with a modified Gauss-Seidel method under similar system conditions. Simulation results show improved convergence characteristics, accurate voltage magnitudes and angles, and frequency estimation, demonstrating that the proposed method is suitable for islanded microgrid practical load flow analysis.

title: Cross-Domain AI Fusion: Integrating Smart Grid Environmental Signals with Diabetes Risk Prediction
authors: Hasan Ahamed Alif, Md. Jisan Mashrafi, Md. Wahiduzzaman, Abtahi Noor, Sebagat Selim, Md. Asif Shahriar
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Sustainable Computing and Smart Systems (ICSCSS)
abstract: This work presents a novel cross-domain artificial intelligence fusion architecture that enhances the prediction of diabetes risks by integrating clinical health data with environmental telemetry data from smart grid infrastructure. In metropolitan areas where climate change and energy instability have significantly impacted the environment, the study acknowledges the increasing effects of environmental stressors on chronic illnesses. An Environmental Stress Score (ESS), a composite statistic, is suggested by the study. Temperature, humidity, power factor, and voltage changes are coupled with standard clinical indicators like blood pressure and blood sugar levels to calculate this score. We extracted 10,000 environmental and clinical records from publicly accessible archives. The following machine learning algorithms were tested: Random Forest, XGBoost, Multilayer Perceptron, and Logistic Regression. The XGBoost model achieved the best accuracy of 0.98 on AUC and an F1-score of 0.94. Even more so than blood pressure and glucose levels, the interpretability research of SHAP demonstrated that ESS is the most potent predictor. Clinical risk modeling may and should incorporate ecological signals, as this fact indicates. The cross-domain solution serves as the basis for future climate-aware real-time health monitoring systems.

title: RISCNet: A Novel Deep Learning Model for Real-Time Radiation induced sickness Classification
authors: Raja Kafi Rahmatullah, Humayra Akhter, Kawshik Das, Md Shamim Ahmed, Ramit Kumar Sadhukhan
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Multidisciplinary Conference on Engineering Technology (IMCET)
abstract: Exposure to ionizing radiation poses significant health risks, leading to a wide spectrum of radiation-induced toxicities, ranging from localized effects like cataracts to systemic acute reactions such as extensive vomiting. The accurate and early classification of these symptoms are critical for effective clinical intervention and patient management. However, hybridity of data and scarcity of balanced exposure datasets presents a significant challenge for traditional diagnostic method. This research introduces the Radiation Induced Sickness Classification Network (RISCNET), an adaptation of the XDNet hybrid machine learning framework, specifically tailored to classify radiation-induced sickness based on dosimetric and clinical features. Unlike generic AI systems, RISCNet is tailored to the unique characteristics of improved accuracy and handling complex data sets originated from radiation induced sickness. The framework combines the Deep Neural Network (DNN) to extract patterns from high-dimensional numerical data, such as absorbed dose and exposure duration, while incorporating CatBoost to handle categorical clinical symptoms and demographic variables. To overcome the inherent limitations of small medical datasets, a Synthetic Data Augmentation Network (SDAN) was utilized to generate a robust training environment. The process starts with gathering input data from cesium-137 isotope around from nuclear weapons testing and from the accidents at Chernobyl and Fukushima. Based on calibration tests of multiple device designs using the same detector), including radiation levels and environmental parameters, followed by preprocessing to clean and normalize the data. The RISCNet model is then trained on a portion of the dataset and tested on 30% to evaluate its performance. The trained model provides accurate radiation-based sickness classification, including real-time data processing. Our suggested model achieved 99.79% accuracy, with same amount of precision and recall, as demonstrated by the confusion matrix for six-class radiation induced sickness classification. These results validate its effectiveness for real-time applications which also outperforms significantly baseline single-architecture models. By providing real-time, interpretable classification of toxicities like ocular damage and gastrointestinal distress, this framework offers a powerful tool for clinicians in radiotherapy. It also enhances safety, reduces costs, and minimizes human error, offering scalable solutions for radiation sickness and other domains. Future developments can include advanced unsupervised techniques, integrating multi-modal 3D radiomic imaging and real-time wearable sensor data to enhance personalized diagnostic precision and clinical explainability in radiological emergencies and multi-modal data handling for broader applicability and real-world deployment.

title: Trends and Concepts in International R&D Organization
authors: Zakariya Chabani, Soumaya Askri, Mohamed Dawood Shamout, Salima Hamouchi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Business Intelligence for Technology Innovation (ICBITI)
abstract: The globalization of research and development (RD) activities has become an industry focus of multinational corporations (MNCs) to foster innovation, competitiveness, and diffusion of knowledge. This research evaluates the models of global RD structures and their changes, focusing on centralization and decentralization ramifications. Centralized RD models offer efficiency, cost savings, and intellectual property protection. However, these centralized systems may be rigid toward serving different regional markets. Decentralized models improve local responsiveness and regionally driven innovation but suffer from coordination, resource, and knowledge management challenges. RD hubs and integrated networks are the dominant hybrid structures in organized science. They enable firms to control strategic decisions while allowing local innovation. Moreover, traditional silos of the past are dissolving owing to innovations in digitalization, AI, and big data, which change RD by allowing collaboration and predictive analysis on management. The analysis highlights the criticality of strong governance, cross-functional collaboration, and open innovation in optimizing global RD systems. In the increasingly interdependent, information-driven economies, the ability of firms to embed themselves into global knowledge networks will define the timeliness and quality of emerging technology adoption at a strategic level while preserving the agility needed to respond proactively to competition over sustained periods.

title: Synchrochain: a Hybrid Ai Architecture for Real-Time Supply Chain Optimization
authors: P Sreejindeth, Padmavathy T
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Trends in Information Technology (ICITIIT)
abstract: Modern supply chains are subject to variable demand, uncertain lead times, and evolving sustainability targets. SynchroChain is presented as a hybrid intelligence layer in which data-driven models provide signal and rule-based guardrails maintain reliability when signals are weak. The system combines: (i) a Transformer that predicts customer intent with 77.3 % accuracy and computes urgency deterministically from the predicted intent, (ii) a lightweight, attention-inspired delayrisk classifier over order-level features (no explicit graph message passing) that reaches 84.13 % precision and a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$65.59 \% ~\mathrm{F} 1$</tex>-score, and (iii) a Proximal Policy Optimization agent that balances cost, service, risk, and carbon under explicit guardrails. A lightweight orchestrator coordinates these components, validates decisions against rules, and provides graceful fallbacks when confidence is low or errors occur. In a simulated CPU-only benchmark harness, the end-to-end pipeline achieves sub- 20 ms median latency, 61.1 requests/s throughput, and 99.87 % successful execution. These results indicate that combining learned signals with conservative rules provides a practical path to dependable, real-time decision support.

title: A Prisma-Based Hyperspectral Dataset for Agriculture
authors: Matei Debu, Kamal Marandskiy, Serban Oprisescu, Mihai Ivanovici
publish: 2025 15th Workshop on Hyperspectral Imaging and Signal Processing: Evolution in Remote Sensing (WHISPERS)
abstract: In this article we introduce a hyperspectral image dataset derived from PRISMA hyperspectral data, entitled HyDACI6. The dataset contains six cropped and filtered hyperspectral images acquired in 2024 over the area of interest to the North of Brasov City in Romania. The images were cropped to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$700 \times 700$</tex> pixels and the spectral bands corresponding to the atmosphere blocking were filtered out, resulting in 187 spectral bands out of the 239 original ones. The dataset contains the mask with the labels of the agricultural crops of the National Institute of Research and Development for Potato and Sugar Beet in Brasov. The HyDACI6 dataset is freely available as open data on Zenodo. We illustrate the usefulness of the proposed dataset with results on an agricultural crop identification application in agriculture.

title: Autonomous Swarm UAVs for Human Detection in Flood Environments using MATLAB Simulation
authors: Shyed Abdul Khadar Jailani H, Arulmozhi K, Anusindhiya K, Santhi S, Sathish Kumar K
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Sustainable Computing and Smart Systems (ICSCSS)
abstract: Flood disasters create immense challenges for search and rescue efforts due to their unpredictable nature and difficult-to-access locations. This study develops an autonomous swarm drone system optimized for locating and identifying stranded individuals in flood-affected areas. By leveraging swarm intelligence and advanced path optimization techniques, the system enhances navigation, search efficiency, and coverage using thermal and acoustic sensor-based simulations. The research focuses on refining swarm path planning through Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO). A MATLAB - based simulation framework is designed to model real-time swarm behavior, integrating environmental complexities such as water currents, obstacles and human movement patterns. Optimization strategies improve route planning, obstacle avoidance, and adaptive search coverage, ensuring minimal energy consumption while maximizing search effectiveness. Performance is assessed based on detection accuracy, response speed, coordination reliability, and computational efficiency. Results demonstrate that PSO and ACO-based swarm control significantly enhance autonomous navigation, search precision, and adaptability compared to conventional search strategies. Optimized swarm paths minimize redundant searches, reduce overall mission time, and improve real-time decision-making in complex flood environments. This work presents a scalable and efficient simulation-driven solution for search and rescue operations, contributing to intelligent UAV coordination frameworks. Future enhancements will explore multi-objective optimization, reinforcement learning techniques, and real-time adaptive swarm path control to further refine disaster response efficiency.

title: Explainable Machine Learning Framework for Multi-Stage Chronic Kidney Disease Classification
authors: Abdul Haque, Nadeem Akhtar, Tameem Ahmad, Mohd Aslam
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)
abstract: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) is a progressive condition that requires accurate staging for effective clinical management and timely intervention. In this work, we propose an explainable machine learning framework for multi-class CKD stage classification using structured clinical data. Experiments were conducted on a large CKD dataset comprising approximately 4,000 patient records with demographic, biochemical, and lifestyle attributes. Six feature configurations were systematically evaluated: (1) GFR only, (2) serum creatinine only, (3) GFR combined with creatinine, (4) top five SHAP-ranked features including creatinine, (5) top four SHAP-ranked features excluding creatinine, and (6) all available features. Multiple machine learning models—including XGBoost, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine, and k-Nearest Neighbors—were implemented, followed by a soft-voting ensemble model. Model performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and class-specific sensitivity and specificity. Experimental results demonstrate that feature sets containing GFR achieved near-perfect classification performance (accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geq 0.96$</tex>), while creatinine alone resulted in poor accuracy (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 0.19$</tex>). SHAP analysis consistently identified GFR as the most influential feature. The findings highlight that GFR is the dominant determinant of CKD stage classification and confirm the suitability of XGBoost-based explainable models for reliable and transparent clinical decision support.

title: A Generative AI–Enhanced Framework for Smart Sustainable Electrical Networks
authors: Mohamed Shaaban, Abdulrahman Al-Shanoon, Mohamad Nassereddine
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Multidisciplinary Conference on Engineering Technology (IMCET)
abstract: High penetration of solar and wind generation introduces significant uncertainty that challenges conventional deterministic power system operation. This paper proposes a unified five-layer Generative-AI-Enhanced Machine Learning framework for managing smart electrical networks under renewable variability. The framework integrates deterministic forecasting, probabilistic scenario generation, physics-informed AC power flow propagation, and voltage feasibility restoration within a single end-to-end workflow. Short-term renewable prediction is performed using an LSTM model for solar power and an XGBoost model for wind generation. A conditional neural scenario generator then expands limited historical data into realistic future trajectories, enabling systematic assessment of uncertainty and rare operating conditions. Monte Carlo scenarios are propagated through a differentiable AC power flow model to identify potential voltage risks, while a physics-aware optimization layer restores feasibility using minimal reactive power support. Case studies based on UAE climate and load data demonstrate that deterministic forecasts alone underestimate network risk, whereas scenario-based analysis reveals up to 18% undervoltage violations before control. After applying the proposed restoration layer, violations are reduced to near-zero levels with reactive adjustments on the order of 0.0001 per unit. The results confirm that the framework enhances risk awareness, improves voltage security, and provides a practical AI-enabled pathway for resilient and interpretable smart grid operation.

title: Leveraging Machine Learning for the Epidemiological Study of Autism Spectrum Disorder
authors: Dinesh Krishnan P, Srinikethan A V, Savitha V, G.Elavel Visuvanathan, Harisudha Kuresan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: The problem of early diagnosis of autism spectrum disorders is a major issue in the neurodevelopment of children. In this study, a comprehensive comparative assessment of five convolutional neural network models is carried out for the risk assessment of autism spectrum disorders using facial image-based approaches under strict integrity conditions. In this study, a previously unknown issue of data leakage in the benchmark facial image dataset is identified and rectified. In this regard, 757 duplicate image filenames are removed from the benchmark facial image dataset before model development. Five models, namely, ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0, MobileNetV2, DenseNet121, and VGG16, are assessed using a benchmark dataset of 2140 images after applying the transfer learning technique and progressive unfreezing. Medical-grade evaluation parameters such as sensitivity, specificity, balanced accuracy, Matthews correlation coefficient, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve are used to assess the performance of the five models. Among the five models, the highest performance is obtained using the ResNet50 model, which shows an accuracy of 78.10 %, an AUC of 0.864, and an MCC of 0.568. An ensemble of the ResNet50 and DenseNet121 models using a soft voting strategy shows an improvement in the AUC to 0.867, which is further enhanced to 0.878. Gradient-weighted class activation mapping was incorporated to enable visual explanations of the predictions made by the model. The results indicate that data leakage correction significantly impacts performance, emphasizing the importance of data partition in medical imaging studies.

title: AI Powered Smart Mobility: Enabling Vision Free Navigation for the Visually Impaired
authors: R. Prakash, V.S Nilauvarasi, R. Sona, R. Sivasakthidevi, E. Sophiya
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS)
abstract: Many people who have impaired vision have difficulty being able to move independently because they have no access to real time awareness of their environment and no safe navigation assistance. While traditional assistive devices like walking sticks and guide dogs can give limited access to the surrounding space, they cannot give complete identification of every obstacle, every person, and every alternative route in a constantly changing environment. There is currently nothing in existence today that allows for true independent mobility. Our project proposes a solution to the existing problem, an AI-driven smart mobility system that enables an individual to navigate freely without any sight using an embedded Raspberry Pi platform. The device utilizes a video camera attached to the Raspberry Pi to continually record the surroundings of the user and employs computer vision techniques using deep learning algorithms to detect objects in the environment, recognize people and provide general assistance throughout the route. Using a training set of data, the software will determine if someone is classified as a known individual or unknown, hence providing contextsensitive audio feedback to the individual/user and thus helping them build confidence in their capacity to independently make decisions. Intelligent obstacle identification technology is also part of the device, which aids users in identifying safe alternate pathways with now increased navigational confidence. Additionally, integrated into the device are two special emergency buttons: One button will allow the user to receive a GPS-enabled location and navigation route to a pre-determined safe destination. The other button allows the user to send an emergency message to pre-arranged contacts, providing real-time location information as well. The combination of AI, GPS, and assistive feedback will help convert complex, visual data into actionable guidance, ultimately increasing safety, independence and situational awareness for visually impaired individuals. In this work, it was suggested that an AI-based smart mobility system be implemented to enable visually impaired people to navigate without vision using embedded computer vision, GPS, and emergency communication modules. The system is capable of real-time object detection, person recognition, and audiobased adaptive route guidance. Experimental analysis in both indoor and outdoor settings shows a detection rate of up to 96 %, person recognition accuracy of 95%, and emergency alert response time below 2.5 seconds. The findings affirm that the perception, navigation, and safety systems are wellintegrated to enhance situational awareness, navigation reliability, and user safety, which is why the proposed system can be used in real-life assistive mobility contexts.

title: Algorithmic Decomposition of ESG Scores: A Sustainability Governance Analysis of Indian Listed Companies
authors: Sadhana S, M. Velavan, Sowmiya R, C. Thirumal Azhagan, Dhandayuthapani Paramasivam
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)
abstract: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) scores have become widely used indicators of corporate sustainability performance. However, the internal structure and informational content of these composite scores remain insufficiently examined, particularly in emerging markets. This study investigates the construction and explanatory structure of ESG scores for Indian listed companies using firm-level ESG data obtained from CRISIL for the year 2024. A diagnostic analytical framework is applied to decompose the composite ESG score into its underlying Environmental (E), Social (S), and Governance (G) components. Using descriptive statistics, correlation analysis, ordinary least squares (OLS) regression, residual diagnostics, and clustering techniques, the study evaluates the extent to which the aggregate ESG score is explained by its components and identifies structural patterns in firm-level ESG performance. Results show that the composite ESG score is largely an aggregation of its underlying components, with Governance exhibiting the highest marginal contribution to the final ESG score. Residual analysis reveals minor deviations between predicted and observed ESG values, suggesting limited but observable rating inefficiencies. Cluster analysis further identifies distinct sustainability profiles among firms rather than a uniform ESG progression. To enhance methodological rigor, robustness checks using Principal Component Analysis (PCA), sector-interaction regression models, and bootstrap validation are proposed to examine stability of coefficients and sectoral heterogeneity. The findings indicate that while ESG scores provide a convenient summary indicator, they primarily represent structured combinations of underlying sustainability indicators rather than independent measures of sustainability performance. The study contributes to ESG analytics by providing a structural decomposition framework that improves interpretability and transparency in sustainability ratings.

title: Optimized Ensemble Learning with SMOTE for Gastric Cancer Classification
authors: Sunipun Seemanta, Mahmudul Haque Shakir, Md. Saef Ullah Miah
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Biomedical Engineering, Computer and Information Technology for Health (BECITHCON)
abstract: Gastric cancer (GC) is a common and mortal cancer worldwide, and the classification of its subtypes can be used as independent factors in determining the prognosis and therapeutic strategies. However, due to overlapping morphological characteristics as well as class imbalance in clinical datasets, it is difficult to distinguish the histological subtypes accurately. The objective of this study is to develop an optimal ensemble learning framework that balances the data through SMOTE and applies a hybrid soft voting ensemble that is formed with Random Forest, XGBoost and Logistic Regression classifiers. This study compare the results between the baseline and ensemble models. The findings indicate a noteworthy enhancement produced by the use of class rebalancing for minority classes. The ensemble model also performed better than all other single models as indicated by an accuracy of 90%. These results demonstrate that ensemble learning integrated with class balancing approach can achieve an accurate and equitable classification of gastric cancer subtypes, contributing to guiding precise and personalized clinical decisions.

title: Automatic Soap Weighing and Shaping System
authors: N. Senthilnathan, Dhaaranesh A, Harsitha G S, Kalaiarasu R, Saran R
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS)
abstract: The soap manufacturing industry requires precise weight and shape control to maintain product uniformity and quality. Manual weighing and shaping processes are time-consuming and susceptible to human error, making them unsuitable for large-scale production. This paper presents an automatic soap weighing and shaping system using a 10 kg load cell with HX711 amplifier for real-time weight measurement. When the measured weight exceeds the predefined <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 5 ~ g}$</tex> limit, a motor-driven trimming mechanism automatically removes excess material. The system is controlled by an Arduino Uno and includes a brush-assisted waste removal unit, with real-time status displayed on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$16 \times 2$</tex> LCD. Experimental results demonstrate consistent trimming accuracy within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 2 ~\mathrm{g}$</tex> and a reduction in processing time compared to manual operation. The proposed system enhances efficiency, reduces labor dependency, and offers potential for future integration with conveyor systems, wireless data monitoring, and AI-enabled shape inspection.

title: Hyperparameter Optimization in Sentiment Analysis Using Reinforcement Learning
authors: N.K. Priyadharsini, R. Kavitha, T. Navin Raaj, P. Kavibharathi, M. Aadith, Gokul Kannan R
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS)
abstract: Hyperparameter optimization has been a vital part of enhancing the performance and generality of Deep Learning models. The existing optimization techniques like grid search and random search are computationally expensive and are incapable of dynamic changes based on feedback from models during training practice. A Hyperparameter Optimization Framework based on Deep Q-Learning that optimizes hyperparameters in a dynamic manner in real-time conditions in stand-alone applications related to sentimental analysis in texts have been designed as a solution for the existing systems issues. The work combines a Reinforcement Learning agent paired with a designed Sentiment Environment (SentimentEnv) that takes validation feedback from models iteratively during training sessions so that hyperparameters are adjusted accordingly in an optimal way. The baseline model used in this experiment is a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) Network based on a pre-trained IMDB movie review dataset that has been effectively used in understanding text segment dependencies in a contextual manner in previous studies related to Natural Language Processing applications. Experimental observations clearly state that this DQN optimization strategy takes fewer training iterations, has a better validation accuracy rate and a stable output relative to existing optimization strategies in this context. The system has been effectively able to conceptualize a synthesis between static optimization algorithms and dynamic adaptations by machines in a way that can be scalable in a completely automated fashion in DL models. The current work lays a credible groundwork in extending hyperparameter optimization in applications related to Reinforcement Learning in computer visions applications, healthcare-related data analyses and bi-temporal predictive analyses in increasing proportions in due course.

title: Inverse Diagnostics for Energy Efficiency: Optimizing Heat Pump Placement in Greenhouses via Gradient-Flux Mapping
authors: Loubna Khaled, Rabih Dib, Bakri Abdulhay, Nazih Moubayed
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Multidisciplinary Conference on Engineering Technology (IMCET)
abstract: The energy footprint of controlled environment agriculture is related to the power demands of microclimate control systems, particularly heat pumps, which are often oversized and inefficiently deployed due to a lack of precise diagnostic tools. This paper discusses the critical challenge of reducing electrical and fuel consumption by introducing a simple, low-computational-demand inverse method for optimizing the placement of system components. The methodology evaluates available multi-point experimental data on temperature and relative humidity stratification in a 332 m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, 3m high, thermal-polyethylene-covered Quonset greenhouse in Lebanon. The simple gradient inverse method is employed to compute the inferred heat flux, the moisture flux, and the density gradient, qualitatively describing the air movement. The results show the accumulation of heat and moisture at the top-far end of the studied structure, and the inferred air movement from low (bottom inlet) to high (top outlet) density areas. This convergence locates the dominant source of thermal stratification and buoyant flow, which is the primary driver of microclimate non-uniformity and energy waste. The derived technical decision is to strategically place the heat pump and vent openings to actively break the buoyant flow, rather than simply treating the average air volume. Also, the use of a variable perforation duct system for homogeneity at the ground (heating) and plant (cooling) levels for optimum uniformity. This work provides a practical, data-driven framework for power system engineers to optimize the deployment of thermal management equipment in agricultural and industrial settings.

title: Regional Education Management Intelligent Construction: A Multi-Modal Fusion Framework Based on Graph Neural Networks
authors: Jie Li
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI in Education Technology and Applications (AIETA)
abstract: In the context of educational digital transformation, regional education management faces significant challenges such as data silos leading to delayed decision-making, uneven resource allocation causing educational inequities, and privacy risks in intelligent tool applications. This study proposes a multi-modal fusion framework based on graph neural networks to address these dilemmas and optimize intelligent construction. The framework employs pre-trained GraphSAGE for encoding regional education data networks, extracting node-level and graph-level features from schools' resources and student performance. BERT processes policy texts and user feedback to generate semantic embeddings. A dynamic fusion layer uses policy semantic embeddings as queries to adjust attention weights on education graph features dynamically, mitigating generalization limitations of static mechanisms. Joint representation learning integrates multihead self-attention, feed-forward networks, graph convolutions, and multi-layer perceptrons, enhanced by a Mixture of Experts (MoE) modification for handling heterogeneous data. Training utilizes mean squared error loss combined with contrastive learning to boost robustness. Experiments on the Students' Performance Dataset demonstrate superior performance, with our model achieving up to 0.95 accuracy, outperforming baselines like MLP, GCN, and GraphSAGE. Results highlight improved decision efficiency, fairness, and privacy safeguards, providing practical strategies for regional education management optimization.

title: Analysis of Lossless Coding Techniques for Uncalibrated Spectral Data From James Webb Space Telescope
authors: Xavier Fernández-Mellado, Pau Quintas-Torra, Joan Bartrina-Rapesta, Jordi Portell, Armando J. Pinho, Joan Serra-Sagristà
publish: 2025 15th Workshop on Hyperspectral Imaging and Signal Processing: Evolution in Remote Sensing (WHISPERS)
abstract: This work evaluates the performance of various lossless compression algorithms on data acquired by the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST). The dataset comprises spectral images from all four of its instruments (NIRCam, NIRISS, NIRSpec, and MIRI), where spectral information is represented spatially because of prism-based dispersion, and includes multiple bands generated through the up-the-ramp readout. We compare compressors that only exploit spatial redundancy -such as JPEG, FPACK variants, LZMA, and Zstandard- and methods that employ spatial and/or spectral redundancy -such as CCSDS 123.0-B-2 and FAPEC (CILLIC and DWT). Experimental results indicate that lossless techniques using spectral redundancy significantly reduce data volume and bits per sample, outperforming purely spatial approaches. In particular, FAPEC-DWT achieves the overall highest compression efficiency, confirming the advantage of incorporating spectral information. These findings support the integration of spectral-aware techniques in future space missions, to maximize data preservation under strict bandwidth constraints.

title: Design and Evaluation of a Hybrid Quantum Model for Intrusion Detection Systems
authors: Sweetha S, Manikandan A, Vijayalakshmi M, Harini Mai V G
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Trends in Information Technology (ICITIIT)
abstract: The paper presents a hybrid structure for an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) designed to enhance cybersecurity by using advanced technologies like Quantum Support Vector Machines (QSVM) and Quantum Neural Networks (QNN). With the rising number of cyberattacks, having effective detection solutions is essential. The proposed model uses quantum neural architecture and quantum kernel methods to identify and classify complex patterns in network traffic. The large-scale CIC DDoS (256,890 records) and UNSW-NB15 (191,694 records) data sets perform better on experimental evaluation. The hybrid quantum-classical system has a maximum accuracy of 99 per cent with the Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) environment, which is better than the traditional models. These findings support the high potential of considering quantum computing to enhance intrusion detection and enhance cyber resilience.

title: Multi-Excitation Hyperspectral Imaging Toward Improved Lichen Identification
authors: Narek Chilingaryan, Arsen Gasparyan, Narine Sarvazyan
publish: 2025 15th Workshop on Hyperspectral Imaging and Signal Processing: Evolution in Remote Sensing (WHISPERS)
abstract: Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) has significantly advanced the identification and characterization of biological specimens by utilizing high-resolution data across multiple spectral bands. In the study of lichens, which are symbiotic entities formed by fungi and photosynthetic partners, our novel approach leverages the potential of HSI through a technique referred to as multi-excitation HSI (ME-HSI). This method differentiates itself by employing multiple ultraviolet wavelengths for illumination, capturing data in the visible range, and subsequently integrating these hyperspectral cubes. Our research demonstrates that this innovative technique provides superior effectiveness in identifying lichen species. By detailing unique spectral signatures, ME-HSI facilitates species differentiation, enhancing the accuracy of ecological monitoring and conservation efforts through non-invasive methods.

title: Enabling Smart Radio Environments in the Frequency Domain With Movable Signals
authors: Matteo Nerini, Bruno Clerckx
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Smart radio environments (SREs) enhance wireless communications by allowing control over the channel. They have been enabled through surfaces with reconfigurable electromagnetic (EM) properties, known as reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs), and through flexible antennas, which can be viewed as realizations of SREs in the EM domain and space domain, respectively. However, these technologies rely on electronically reconfigurable or movable components, introducing implementation challenges that could hinder commercialization. To overcome these challenges, we propose a new domain to enable SREs, the frequency domain, through the concept of movable signals, where the signal spectrum can be dynamically moved along the frequency axis. We first analyze movable signals in multiple-input single-output (MISO) systems under line-of-sight (LoS) conditions, showing that they can achieve higher average received power than quantized equal gain transmission (EGT). We then study movable signals under non-line-of-sight (NLoS) conditions, showing that they remain effective by leveraging reflections from surfaces made of uniformly spaced elements with fixed EM properties, denoted as fixed intelligent surfaces (FISs). Analytical results reveal that a FIS-aided system using movable signals can achieve up to four times the received power of a RIS-aided system using fixed-frequency signals.

title: Uncovering Alzheimer’s Disease Patterns in Patient Health Records through Artificial Intelligence and Principal Component Analysis
authors: Agung Muliawan, Lama Yassine, Achmad Rizal, Amira J. Zaylaa
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Multidisciplinary Conference on Engineering Technology (IMCET)
abstract: Alzheimer’s disease is a form of progressive dementia that is often detected at an advanced stage, making early intervention and effective management difficult. This study explores the integration of artificial neural networks (ANN) with ensemble models for early classification of Alzheimer’s disease by utilizing an open dataset of patient health parameters. This study explores the integration of deep learning and neural network approach with ensemble classifier to analyze health data, including indicators of hypertension, cholesterol, diabetes, heart disease, weight, and family history. A deep neural network (DNN) model is employed on a dataset covering patients with various stages of the disease, allowing the network to learn complex patterns and important features associated with Alzheimer’s progression. In addition, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was used for dimension reduction without reducing information, and the ensemble classifier, specifically the Bagging and Boosting models, were integrated for increasing optimization of Alzheimer’s disease classification accuracy. The results applied on real patient’s records showed that the use of feedforward neural network (FNN) with PCA was advantageous as compared to the use of the same network without PCA, with 0.87% increase in the accuracy of detection of the disease. Integrating FNN with Boosting model using PCA showed the utmost and optimal early detection accuracy (83.95%) and was approximately 2% higher than that obtained using the deep learning without PCA (81.24%). However, the difference in the accuracy of detection while integrating the deep learning with the ensemble models was in the range of 1 to 1.5% higher than the difference in the accuracy using FNN with ensemble models. This study suggests that applying artificial intelligence techniques can be valuable in early diagnosis efforts, facilitating faster intervention and better care strategies for Alzheimer’s patients.

title: Rover Based Autonomous Waste Segregation and Donation System
authors: Sanjay Ashok, Radhika P, Vytla Shreya, Sai Ruthvik
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: The current paper encompasses design and development, as well as implementation of an autonomous robot, integrating computer vision based on YOLOv8, sensor-based navigation and the IoT-enabled smart waste segregation monitoring. The system proposed uses the ESP32 microcontroller as the central processing unit connecting with the ultrasonic sensors to detect the obstacles and avoid them, a web camera to take realtime images of the waste, and servo motors to control clamps precisely. Quick and successful learning A YOLOv8 model is trained using a personal dataset of organic and inorganic waste materials with a training accuracy of 99 % and loss of 0.15 after eight epochs. TCP protocol is used to provide effective communication between the ESP32 and the vision processing unit, which is written in Python. Moreover, a dustbin fill sensor is used to keep track of the fill level of dustbins, and the data is sent to Firebase, which is utilized to provide real-time cloud-based monitoring, which can be accessed through an App Inventor interface of MIT. The system is proven workable through experimental findings concerning autonomous navigation, waste classification, and IoT integration to show a smooth end-to-end functionality. This study adds a holistic model of independent waste management, and its implications to smart city applications and sustainable living in cities are enormous.

title: Structured Distilled 3D Gait Fields for Clothes-Changing Person Re-Identification in Videos
authors: Yubin Wang, Huimin Yu, Haoji Hu, Yongsheng Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: 3D gait representation, intrinsically tied to identity, is of great importance in tackling the challenge of video-based clothes-changing person re-identification (VCCRe-ID), where individuals’ clothing appearances vary across video clips captured over an extended duration. Most existing methods derive 3D gait features from sequential 3D skeletons or parameters of a predefined 3D human model (e.g. SMPL). However, the sparsity of 3D skeletons and the low-dimensional nature of 3D human model parameters not only limit their expressive power and discriminative ability, but also impede alignment with higher-dimensional appearance features. To address these issues, we propose Structured Distilled 3D Gait Fields (SD-3DGF) to learn better aligned, more expressive, and discriminative 3D gait features for VCCRe-ID. SD-3DGF defines distilled feature fields that evolve over time, allowing the simulation of 3D gait dynamics from temporal 2D descriptors. Specifically, it densely transfers frame-level shape knowledge from structured 3D space (i.e., 3D mesh surface) to the 2D image domain via 3D-2D knowledge distillation, and models local spatiotemporal correlations among the distilled features to extract 3D gait features. This learning paradigm makes 3D gait features more compatible with appearance features, as they are naturally aligned in the spatiotemporal domain. To fully exploit this potential, we develop a novel Modality-Representative Interactive Encoding (MRIE) module, which facilitates efficient gait-appearance interaction and joint encoding via global and local representative tokens. Experiments on both VCCRe-ID and standard video-based Re-ID benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. The code is available at https://github.com/yubinwang2021/SD-3DGF.

title: Robust Vehicle and Pedestrian Detection in Adverse Weather Via a Weather-Aware Transformer
authors: Xingquan Cai, Jiawei Tang, Hongli Ding, Yuanxiang Zhao, Ying Li
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Pedestrian and vehicle in adverse weather detection technology based on deep learning has attracted increasing attention. However, YOLO-based detectors degrade under low contrast and noise, causing missed small or occluded targets, while DETR-based models suffer from slow convergence and limited multi-scale sensitivity. To address these challenges, a robust weather-aware transformer for vehicle and pedestrian detection, RWT-DETR, is proposed. Firstly, a Strip-Oriented Feature Gating (SOFG) module is designed to improve the capture of strip-like and directional features of traffic targets. Secondly, a Signed Attention Decomposition (SAD) mechanism is designed to enable the encoder to model both positive and negative correlations, thereby enhancing the model's robustness against noise interference and feature blurring. Finally, an Adaptive Multi-scale Fusion with Gating (AMFG) module is proposed to achieve dynamic calibration and denoising of cross-scale features, significantly improving semantic alignment. Experimental results show that, compared with the baseline, the proposed method improves mAP@0.5 and mAP@0.5:0.95 by 2.4% and 3.0%, respectively. Meanwhile, it outperforms existing mainstream models, confirming its effectiveness under adverse weather conditions.

title: Classifier Weighted Mixture models
authors: Elouan Argouarc'h, Franc¸ois Desbouvries, Eric Barat, Eiji Kawasaki, Thomas Dautremer
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: This paper proposes an extension of standard mixture stochastic models, by replacing the constant mixture weights with functional weights defined using a classifier. Classifier Weighted Mixtures enable straightforward density evaluation, explicit sampling, and enhanced expressivity in variational estimation problems, without increasing the number of components nor the complexity of the mixture components.

title: Uncertainty Quantification for Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models: Foundations, Methodology, and Future Directions
authors: Sungmin Kang, Yavuz Faruk Bakman, Duygu Nur Yaldiz, Baturalp Buyukates, Salman Avestimehr
publish: IEEE BITS the Information Theory Magazine
abstract: The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has transformed the landscape of natural language processing, enabling breakthroughs across a wide range of areas including question answering, machine translation, and text summarization. Yet, their deployment in real-world applications has raised concerns over reliability and trustworthiness, as LLMs remain prone to hallucinations that produce plausible but factually incorrect outputs. Uncertainty quantification (UQ) has emerged as a central research direction to address this issue, offering principled measures for assessing the trustworthiness of model generations. We begin by introducing the foundations of UQ, from its formal definition to the traditional distinction between epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty, and then highlight how these concepts have been adapted to the context of LLMs. Building on this, we examine the role of UQ in hallucination detection, where quantifying uncertainty provides a mechanism for identifying unreliable generations and improving reliability. We systematically categorize a wide spectrum of existing methods along multiple dimensions and present empirical results for several representative approaches. Finally, we discuss current limitations and outline promising future research directions, providing a clearer picture of the current landscape of LLM UQ for hallucination detection.

title: Brain Tumor Classification in MRI Images: A Computationally Efficient Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Md Fahimul Kabir Chowdhury, Jannatul Ferdous
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Biomedical Engineering, Computer and Information Technology for Health (BECITHCON)
abstract: Improving patient outcomes depends on the prompt and accurate diagnosis of brain tumors, but manual MRI scan analysis is still time-consuming and unreliable. Although deep learning has shown promise, many of the models that are now in use are computationally intensive and have difficulty handling the intrinsic complexity and variety of different types of brain tumors. In this work, we propose a lightweight yet high-performing Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for multiclass brain tumor classification, employing MRI images to target gliomas, meningiomas, pituitary tumors, and healthy (no tumor) instances. The model was rigorously evaluated on two publicly accessible datasets from Figshare and Kaggle. Leveraging efficient feature extraction and optimized training strategies, our CNN achieved classification accuracies of 99.03% and 99.28%, along with ROC scores of 99.88% and 99.94% on Dataset 1 and Dataset 2, respectively-all while utilizing significantly fewer parameters than popular pre-trained architectures. In contrast to cutting-edge models like DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, VGG19, Xception, InceptionV3, and ResNet50, our approach consistently demonstrated superior performance with reduced computational overhead. These findings highlight the potential of the proposed model as a practical and reliable diagnostic aid in clinical environments.

title: Deep Learning-based Volition Detection and Action Potential Extraction for Fully Automated Diagnosis of Neuromuscular Disease Using Needle Electromyography Signals
authors: Soomin Chung, Ilhan Yoo, Jinkyu Lee, Dongmin Kim, Kwangsoo Kim, Keewon Kim, Seung-Bo Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Objective: This study aimed to develop a deep learning-based volition-detection model to automate the diagnostic process and improve neuromuscular disease classification using needle electromyography (nEMG) signals. Methods: The model was developed using 376 nEMG signals from 57 subjects and independently evaluated on an external dataset of 751 nEMG signals from 115 subjects at a single tertiary medical center.
