title: TriSense-AttentionNet: A CNN-LSTM with Multi-Head Attention Fusion Model for Infant Cry Classification
authors: B Satheesh, Kumaresan Angappan
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Deep learning has significant promise in automated infant cry analysis for early health assessment in neonatal care. However, existing approach faces two major limitations. First, most methods rely on single audio representations, missing complementary acoustic information across different domains. Second, temporal dependencies in cry sequences are often inadequately captured, reducing classification reliability. To overcome these challenges, this paper introduces Trisense-AttentionNet, a multimodal frame-work that combines parallel feature extraction with attention-based temporal modeling. The architecture simultaneously processes spectrograms, cochleagrams, and chomograms through dedicated CNN-LSTM pathways, capturing frequency, auditory, and harmonic characteristics respectively. Multihead attention layers identify diagnostically critical cry segments across time. Features from all three pathways undergo concatenation before final classification through dense layers. Compared to the existing deep learning models with single audio representations, experiments on the Baby Chillanto Dataset demonstrate superior performance, achieving 94.73% accuracy, 91.30% precision, 92.58% recall, and 91.52% F1-score across five cry types: Asphyxia, Deaf, Hunger, Normal, and Pain. Notably, the proposed approach outperforms the baseline CNN model by 3.74% and the CNN–LSTM model by 0.66%, highlighting the effectiveness of multi-representation feature fusion and attention mechanisms. The results demonstrate that the suggested model can effectively reduce the model overfitting problem caused by a small dataset. The proposed method fused features outperforms existing methods based on single domain features.

title: Spatial, Climate, and Physics Effects in Global DNI Prediction: A Reproducible Large-Scale Study with Computational Profiling
authors: Dominik Halvoník, Kacper Książek, Małgorzata Przybyła-Kasperek, Martin Drlík, Ján Skalka
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a global framework for predicting Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) that examines how climate conditions, spatial neighbourhoods, physical information, and model architecture influence forecasting accuracy. A unified twelve-zone climate classification, combining the Köppen–Trewartha and WorldSeasons systems, is used to select 36 representative locations worldwide. For each site, four grid topologies and four spatial resolutions are generated, resulting in 2,052 independent nodes. Each node is modelled under five prediction modes – from pure machine learning to physics-only – using two neural architectures: multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and temporal convolutional networks (TCNs). In total, 51,300 model instances are evaluated, including 49,248 trainable learning runs across architectures and splits. The results show that adding physical information, especially air mass, brings the largest improvement and reduces median RMSE by around 25–30 W/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Differences between MLP and TCN are small and vary by climate zone; temporal modelling improves accuracy only slightly while requiring over six times more training time. Higher spatial resolution clearly improves performance, whereas the specific grid shape plays a minor role. Overall, the study indicates that combining physically meaningful features with efficient architectures provides the best balance between accuracy and computational cost. The proposed global framework offers a transparent and reproducible basis for future research in hybrid and climate-aware solar irradiance forecasting and reports a concise computational audit of the pipeline (training volume, storage, and I/O), providing a transparent and reproducible basis for extending the benchmark and for deployment-oriented model selection.

title: Energy Scheduling of Islanded Microgrid Based on Hybrid Optimization Algorithm
authors: Bojun Tan
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Against the backdrop of energy resource scarcity and growing environmental protection demands, distributed energy supply technologies-especially renewable energy technologies-have attracted significant attention due to their high energy efficiency, favorable economic performance, and flexible installation. For remote areas such as plateaus, border defense zones, and isolated islands where the public power supply grid is inaccessible, islanded microgrids have emerged as a key technology to address the challenge of inadequate electricity access. However, achieving in-depth optimization of energy management efficiency remains a major ongoing challenge. This paper focuses on the optimal scheduling problem of an islanded microgrid system integrating photovoltaic equipment, wind power equipment, diesel generation equipment, and energy storage devices. The proposed model takes the minimization of comprehensive operation costs as its core objective, while balancing economic efficiency and environmental protection requirements. It also comprehensively incorporates constraints such as system power balance and output limits of each component. To address the issues of the standard Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm-including its tendency to premature convergence and insufficient convergence performance-a hybrid strategy integrating the Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) algorithm, namely the Particle Swarm-Grey Wolf Optimization (PSO-GWO) algorithm, is proposed to enhance both global exploration and local exploitation capabilities. Simulation analysis is conducted based on typical daily load data, and the results demonstrate that the proposed hybrid algorithm can effectively reduce the system's comprehensive costs and improve the effectiveness of scheduling optimization.

title: Listening to the System: Consumer Complaints as Early Warning Signals
authors: Jennifer Zhang, Reginald Bailey
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Consumer complaints often reflect emerging operational issues before they are captured in financial or performance metrics. However, financial institutions typically use complaint data reactively for case resolution rather than as structured early warning signals for managerial decision-making. This study develops a framework that treats consumer complaints as a sensing mechanism within the consumer-facing operational risk monitoring architecture of financial institutions. Complaint narratives and metadata from the U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau $(C F P B)$ are analyzed to generate complaint-derived features such as topic indicators, recurrence concentration, sentiment volatility, and narrative complexity. These features are evaluated against futureperiod downstream operational strain indicators. This framework aims to demonstrate when complaint-derived signals provide support for issue prioritization and proactive operational intervention.

title: Research on Cascading Failure Blocking Strategies for Highway Energy Systems Considering Load Classification
authors: Penghan Jin, Biao Wang, Hao Wu, Yu Han
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: In the context of highway operation and maintenance, critical loads have low tolerance for power outages, necessitating a security defense system adapted to the new traffic power system. This paper proposes a graded-load-based cascading failure blocking control method, which integrates cascading failure analysis with multi-stage blocking control. Initial faults are selected based on voltage fluctuations, load rates, and branch power ratios. The functional indicators include flow limit violations and power transfer efficiency, while the structural indicators are line betweenness and network average efficiency. A weight optimization strategy combining the Analytic Hierarchy Process and the Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution generates the failure paths. Under a multi-stage blocking strategy, fault propagation is mitigated by adjusting generator outputs, curtailing renewable energy sources, and shedding loads, aiming to minimize the failure probability and loss costs. Results show that the proposed method can reduce the failure probability by approximately 60 %, whereas ignoring the graded-load constraint may lead to excessive curtailment of critical loads, resulting in a 35 % increase in system losses. Case studies validate the applicability and effectiveness of the method, providing both theoretical and practical guidance for preventing cascading failures in highway power systems.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Rice Leaf Disease Detection and Classification Using DenseNet201 and YOLOv8
authors: Molla Md. Sabit, Sabrina Afrin, Mardia Yasmin Muna, Fariha Nizam Prova, Sudipta Bain, Khushnur Binte Jahangir
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Rice, a chief ingredient for over half of the global population and a staple in Bangladesh and other rice-dependent regions, is highly vulnerable to diseases such as bacterial leaf blight, brown spot, and blast, which threaten yield and food security. To address the limitations of manual diagnosis, this study presents a two-step deep learning framework integrating DenseNet201 for classification and YOLOv8 for real-time detection. DenseNet201 achieved 95.0% accuracy, while YOLOv8 reached 96.5% accuracy with an mAP@0.5 of 96.1% and an F1-score of 92.9%. A hybrid ensemble further improved performance, achieving 96.21% accuracy across CPU and GPU, showing more consistent results than individual models. Unlike prior works that focus only on classification or detection, our method combines both in a scalable, cost-effective, and fieldready framework with potential integration into IoT and drone platforms for early disease management and precision agriculture.

title: Generative AI in Systems Engineering: A Framework for Risk Assessment of Large Language Models
authors: Stefan Otten, Philipp Reis, Philipp Rigoll, Tobias Schürmann, Joshua Ransiek, Jacob Langner, Eric Sax
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: The increasing use of Large Language Models (LLMs) offers significant opportunities across the engineering lifecycle, including requirements engineering, software development, process optimization, and decision support. Despite this potential, organizations face substantial challenges in assessing the risks associated with LLM use, resulting in inconsistent integration, unknown failure modes, and limited scalability.This paper introduces the LLM Risk Assessment Framework (LRF), a structured approach for evaluating the application of LLMs within Systems Engineering (SE) environments. The framework classifies LLM-based applications along two fundamental dimensions: autonomy, ranging from supportive assistance to fully automated decision making, and impact, reflecting the potential severity of incorrect or misleading model outputs on engineering processes and system elements. By combining these dimensions, the LRF enables a consistent determination of the corresponding risk levels throughout the development lifecycle.The resulting classification supports organizations in identifying appropriate validation strategies, levels of human oversight, and required countermeasures to ensure safe and transparent deployment. The framework thus helps align the rapid evolution of AI technologies with established engineering principles of reliability, traceability, and controlled process integration. Overall, the LRF provides a basis for risk-aware adoption of LLMs in complex engineering environments and represents a first step toward standardized AI assurance practices in systems engineering.

title: Benchmarking Classical and Quantum Optimization Approaches for Rider-Order Assignment
authors: Tharrmashastha SAPV, Surya Prakash Palanivel, Jasjyot Singh Gulati, M Maruthu Pandi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The logistics industry is widely regarded as a promising application domain for emerging optimization paradigms, including quantum computing. The Rider-Order Assignment problem is a practically motivated optimization problem arising in online food delivery and related logistics applications. While the problem is closely related to the classical matching problem, the inclusion of realistic operational constraints renders it computationally challenging. In this work, we formulate the Rider-Order Assignment problem as a constrained binary optimization problem and perform a comparative analysis of classical, quantum-inspired, and gate-based quantum solvers for this problem across multiple instance sizes. Solver performance is assessed using solution quality, computational runtime, and constraint satisfaction, with a consistent post-processing procedure applied to ensure feasibility.

title: Research on the Development Law of Partial Discharge Caused by Capacitor Plate Tip Defects in Resin Impregnated Paper Bushings
authors: Jufang Wei, Junji Feng, Liqing Liu, Yuxuan Che, Mingyu Wen, Yong Ma, Bo Qi
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the partial discharge development issue caused by capacitor plate tip defects during the manufacturing process of epoxy resin impregnated paper (RIP) bushings, this paper systematically investigates the discharge evolution law of defects under AC voltage through experimental simulation and theoretical analysis. A stepped voltage application method was adopted, combined with pulse current detection technology. A test platform for discharge development of typical defects was established, and resin impregnated paper samples containing plate tip defects were prepared. The temporal evolution of characteristic parameters such as discharge phase, repetition rate, total discharge amount, and average discharge amount was analyzed. Experimental results indicate that the partial discharge development of plate tip defects can be divided into three stages: the initial stage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0-60 ~{\min}$</tex>, sparse discharge, low repetition rate), the development stage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(60-180 ~{\min}$</tex>, intensified discharge, spectrum showing a “wing-like” shape shifting towards the vertical direction), and the hazardous stage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 8 0 - 2 1 0} \mathbf{~ m i n}$</tex>, severe discharge, spectrum evolving into a “rectangular” shape, total discharge amount reaching <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.975 \times 10^{8} \text{pC}$</tex>). Simulation and experimental data show that electric field concentration at the tip is the main cause of breakdown, with the maximum field strength increasing significantly with voltage (reaching <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$245.7 \text{kV} / \text{mm}$</tex> at 30 kV). The study reveals the progressive expansion mechanism of the discharge defect and proposes a discharge stage division method based on the temporal characteristics of multiple parameters. This provides a basis for bushing insulation status assessment and early fault warning, holding significant engineering application value.

title: AC Time-Dependent Dielectric Breakdown in Polymer-Based Galvanic Isolators: Evidence of an Impact of the Electromechanical Stress
authors: M. Greatti, G. L. Scarpiello, T. Singh, F. Speroni, D. Asnaghi, M. Salina, M. Lauria, F. Guzzi, D. Paci, V. Marano, A. S. Spinelli, C. Monzio Compagnoni, G. Malavena
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: We present a wafer-level experimental investigation on the AC Time-Dependent Dielectric Breakdown (TDDB) in galvanic isolators based on polymeric dielectrics. The collected evidence clearly indicates that the electromechanical stress involved in AC operation is a key degradation mechanism for the devices, and it can even become the dominant factor limiting device lifetime under certain operating conditions. The phenomenon is shown to be triggered by the change of polarity of the stress voltage and its main dependences are carefully explored, paying special attention to frequency and temperature. Results highlight that keeping electromechanical stress under control during the on-field operation of the devices is mandatory for the development of highly reliable polymer-based galvanic isolators.

title: Research on Vibration Acoustic Imaging Technology for Ultra-high Voltage Oil-immersed Shunt Reactors
authors: Zhaoliang Gu, Mengzhao Zhu, Wenbing Zhu, Qingdong Zhu, Xucheng Wang, Jian Wang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This paper focuses on the vibration characteristics of ultra-high voltage (UHV) oil-immersed shunt reactors and proposes an improved vibration acoustic imaging method based on a frequency-domain spectral estimation optimization algorithm. Firstly, this paper constructs a microphone array near-field sound source signal model and derives a general array transfer function applicable to scenarios where the sound source distance is uncertain. Subsequently, by dividing the microphone array into multiple overlapping microphone sub-arrays, sound source localization calculations are performed separately. Then, the imaging results of multiple sub-arrays and the full array are fused and normalized, proposing a frequency-domain spectral estimation optimization algorithm. Furthermore, this paper analyzes the impact of array size on imaging performance through a simulation system, clarifying the nonlinear relationship between the number of microphones, main lobe resolution, and side lobe suppression effect. It explicitly proposes that a 64 -element rectangular array is the optimal configuration for this application scenario and is superior to existing methods in key parameters such as aperture area and maximum side lobe height. The relevant research results and data in this paper can provide new ideas and theoretical support for on-site vibration acoustic imaging detection of UHV oil-immersed shunt reactors.

title: Origin of Deep Levels Responsible for Transconductance Overshoot in AlGaN/GaN HEMTs and Impact in Real-Life Operating Conditions
authors: Andrea Carlotto, Francesco De Pieri, Isabella Rossetto, Carlo De Santi, Manuel Fregolent, Fabiana Rampazzo, Matteo Meneghini, Gaudenzio Meneghesso, Enrico Zanoni
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: Dispersion effects are characterized in GaN HEMTs with AlGaN back-barrier (with different Al molar fraction), as compared to samples with a Fe-doped GaN buffer. Drain Current Transient Spectroscopy (DCTS) is used for a systematic characterization of deep levels. Structures with similar buffer thickness are used to provide a proper comparison of conventional and back-barrier devices in equal testing conditions. The following results are discussed: (i) an anomalous peak of the transconductance vs Vgs curve (“ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{m}}$</tex> overshoot”) is detected on both structures and correlated with photoionization effects. (ii) We identify the deep level responsible for the “gm overshoot” and demonstrate that its origin is a native GaN-defect. (iii) Dispersion is found to be correlated with the increase in Al fraction if devices adopt an AlGaN back-barrier. (iv) We propose an innovative analysis, based on a frequency sweep method, which allows the evaluation of the deep levels effect under operating conditions. (v) We demonstrate that the trap responsible for “ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{m}}$</tex> overshoot” induces no instability in operating conditions since de-trapping occurs for longer times. Nevertheless, the influence of trap levels during trapping phase, even for short times (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 1 \mu \mathrm{s}$</tex>), leads to a strong reduction in drain current and consequent impact on the device performance.

title: On-Off Keyed Wireless Quantum Optical Communications via Turbulent Channels with Decoherence
authors: Peter Jung, Kushtrim Dini
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Recent research has given rise to the conjecture that wireless quantum optical communications should be considered as an attractive means of implementing future, e.g. sixth generation (6G), wireless networks in the case of timeinvariant quantum communication channels. In this manuscript the effect of on-off keyed wireless quantum optical communications via the time-varying quantum communication turbulence channel in the presence of thermal background radiation is studied for the first time. The simulation of the stochastic turbulence process, characterizing the time-varying quantum communication turbulence channel and yielding realizations of the average number of signal photons prevailing at the input of the quantum receiver, will be introduced and discussed. In this context, it will be illustrated that the turbulence exhibits log-normal fading. Also, the maximum-likelihood (ML) quantum data detector applicable to the case of quantum reception via the time-varying quantum communication turbulence channel will be given. Finally, simulation results of the bit error ratio (BER) obtained for several representative transmission scenarios will be presented. It is found that the said simulation results support the above-mentioned conjecture regarding future wireless networks.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning Algorithms for Predicting Depression in Bangladeshi University Students
authors: Mohammed Aftab Uddin Alif, Mamun Ahmed, MD.Yeakub Ali, Rahyan Shamsi
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: This study presents a comparative analysis of machine learning algorithms to predict depression among Bangladeshi university students, a population increasingly vulnerable to mental health issues due to academic, social, and financial pressures. Utilizing data collected from 927 undergraduate students through a culturally adapted Bangla version of the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (DASS-21), the study examined key variables including family history of mental illness, academic stress, financial hardship, and interpersonal relationships. Five machine learning models—Logistic Regression (LR), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Random Forest (RF), Decision Tree (DT), and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)—were implemented to develop classification models capable of identifying individuals at risk of depression. The CNN model achieved the highest predictive accuracy at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{9 1 \%}$</tex>, followed by Random Forest at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{8 5 \%}$</tex>, Logistic Regression at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{8 2 \%}$</tex>, and both Decision Tree and KNN at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{7 5 \%}$</tex>. The superior performance of CNN suggests that deep learning techniques are particularly effective in capturing complex patterns within mental health-related datasets. The study further emphasizes the importance of using culturally relevant and linguistically appropriate assessment tools, such as the Bangla DASS-21, to ensure accurate and sensitive data collection within the local context. Importantly, the findings highlight the potential of machine learning to support early intervention strategies in university mental health systems. By leveraging these predictive models, educational institutions and mental health professionals can identify at-risk students more effectively, allowing timely support and reducing the likelihood of severe psychological outcomes. Overall, this research demonstrates the critical role of AI-driven tools in advancing mental health care among university populations in Bangladesh

title: Applying Systems Thinking to the United States Army Corps of Engineers’ Engineering Management System Implementation
authors: Patrick J. Lupfer, Andrew S. Farrant
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: The United States Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) Charleston District is evaluating the implementation of the Engineering Management System (EMS), a headquarters-directed web-based software platform that is intended to standardize workload planning, cost/schedule tracking, and engineering resource management across all districts. Although EMS offers enterprise-wide benefits, district leadership expressed concerns regarding its applicability to Charleston’s unique project portfolio, customer base, and existing business processes. The team partnered with the Charleston District to apply the Systems Decision Process (SDP) as a structured, stakeholder-focused approach to assess value, feasibility, and risks associated with EMS implementation for the district. The project identified the district’s essential needs, evaluated EMS’ features against those needs, and measured potential benefits and startup costs through deliberate problem definition, value modeling, and alternative generation. Extensive stakeholder interviews and system mapping not only highlighted areas in which EMS aligns with the Charleston District’s mission requirements, but also identified areas requiring customization, process redesign, or third-party solutions. The effort produced an evidence-based recommendation to district leadership regarding whether to fully adopt EMS as-is, pursue tailored configuration, or implement supplemental processes outside the platform. The results demonstrate how the SDP provides a clear and repeatable framework for software adoption decisions in engineering organizations. Additionally, it displays how USACE can apply systems thinking to navigate enterprise-level requirements while maintaining local mission effectiveness.

title: Die of Interest – Die Level Predictive Modeling to Reduce Reliability Defect Escape
authors: Chun-Ting Liu, Sing Hee Wong, Wei-Hsun Lin, Yu-Chen Chou, Wan-Ling Tsai, Kam Heng Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: Reliability is a critical quality indicator in semiconductor manufacturing, especially for automotive and high-performance computing products, where latent defects can lead to catastrophic failures. Accurately identifying potential outlier dies while minimizing false positives and optimizing cost efficiency remains a constant challenge. This paper introduces a novel two-stage methodology to address this issue. In the first stage, statistical analysis and machine learning techniques are applied to semiconductor inline data to identify and rank dies exhibiting abnormal patterns, referred to as Die of Interest (DoI). The second stage is envisioned to apply adaptive testing, targeted stress testing, and reliability screening to these outlier dies identified by the Die of Interest framework, thereby ensuring comprehensive evaluation and enhanced reliability. Experimental results demonstrate a 9-fold improvement in identifying outlier dies compared to baseline, highlighting the effectiveness of this approach. The proposed methodology offers a promising solution for achieving superior reliability in semiconductor manufacturing while enhancing efficiency and reducing costs.

title:
ORION-V
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    X: A Standards-Aligned Multi-Plane Architecture for Resilient NTN-Enhanced Vehicular Communications
authors: Bhagwan Das, Nawaz Ali, Gianluca Aloi, Pasquale Pace, Magnus Johnsson, Ali Hassan Sodhro
publish: IEEE Communications Standards Magazine
abstract: Autonomous vehicles are moving beyond controlled environments into large-scale deployments where GNSS degradation, congestion, and infrastructure disruption challenge predictable V2X operation. While 3GPP Release 18 and Release 19 provide key capabilities for NR-V2X and NTN, they do not define how these should be operationally combined for safety-critical use. This article introduces ORION-V2X, a standards-aligned multi-plane framework that composes existing 3GPP capabilities without adding new interfaces. It separates time-critical local safety from edge coordination and wide-area resilience, anchors coordination at RSU micro-edge with fixed deadlines, and uses positioning integrity as a system-wide control input. NTN is integrated strictly as a resilience and coordination layer, not a real-time safety channel. Evaluation under GNSS denial, sidelink congestion, and coverage loss shows contract-level performance with bounded latency, deadline adherence, and stable operational modes. ORION-V2X provides a practical, deployment-oriented reference for industry to realise predictable and resilient NTN-enhanced V2X systems aligned with Release 18/19 evolution.

title: CAFE-Net: Context-Aware Feature Enhancement for Reliable Multi-Scale Detection of Martian Craters
authors: Zhuohang Li, Xinyao Wang, Zhaozhao Zhang, Tongtong Shen, Wen Zheng
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Reliable recognition of Martian impact craters is critical for spacecraft landing site selection and future surface exploration. However, this task remains challenging due to the pronounced multi-scale characteristics of craters and interference from crater-like structures such as Martian domes. Notably, the ejecta textures surrounding craters provide important discriminative cues for distinguishing craters from domes. To address these challenges, a Context-Aware and Feature Enhanced Net (CAFE-Net) is proposed for accurate Martian crater detection in complex environments. CAFE-Net incorporates three key components: a Multi-Scale Context Aware (MSCA) module to capture local texture information, a Feature Enhancement Unit (FEU) to alleviate the loss of small-scale features, and a channel self-learning fusion strategy to strengthen feature representation via channel-wise interactions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves improvements of 3.94% in precision and 3.13% in recall, with an F1-score of 86.41%, outperforming existing methods while maintaining efficient inference. However, the proposed method is currently limited in handling nighttime imagery and severely weathered craters. Future work will explore multi-source data fusion to improve robustness and support applications such as vision-based landing site selection for Mars missions.

title: Further on Novel Multi-State Coupling and Proportional Distributed Delays in Synchronization of Uncertain Complex-Valued State-Dependent Neural Networks for Secure Communication
authors: Yuxian Guo, Liqun Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper investigates the global polynomial synchronization (GPS) of a class of uncertain complex-valued state-dependent neural networks (CVSDNNs) with proportional delays and multi-state coupling via an adaptive event-triggered controller. This work further investigates the synchronization of neural networks (NNs) with proportional distributed delays, as well as the first analysis based on a multi-state coupled NNs (CNNs) model. To effectively handle the challenges posed by proportional distributed delays, we establish two novel integral inequalities developed by exploiting the properties of both the proportional delays and polynomial functions, and construct an appropriate Lyapunov functional. Subsequently, the Intermediate Value Theorem is employed to prove that the Zeno behavior is precluded under the proposed synchronization control. Finally, numerical simulations are conducted not only to validate the theoretical results but also to demonstrate applications in secure communication.

title: Diabetic Retinopathy Segmentation and Feature Extraction using Federated Learning-based Machine Learning Algorithm
authors: R.Ashok Kumar, N. Thillaiarasu
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Sustainable Computing and Smart Systems (ICSCSS)
abstract: Classical techniques of machine learning for segmentation and feature extraction in Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) often involve collecting and storing patient records in a central computing system. This raises not only privacy but also security issues. Hence, in this research, Federated Learning (FL) solves this problem by enabling several medical organizations of choice to train the models while the patient’s data is still stored within a single institution. In the beginning, the data is collected from the benchmark source that has been referred to as Asia Pacific Tele Ophthalmology Society 2019 Blindness Detection (APTOS 2019). Then pre-processing of these collected images is done by Contrast Limit Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE). These preprocessed images undergo the segmentation using the watershed segmentation technique. The last step entails feature extraction from those segmented images using the Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) technique. Moreover, this method reduces the necessity for data transfers which ensures that the privacy of the patients is maintained. Hence, the FL-based LDA not only propels research and development in medical imaging but also enables fair distribution of advanced diagnostic devices, thereby improving the DR management for the patients.

title: Conference Committees
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)

title: Efficient Edge Caching in Data Centers Using Latent Dirichlet Allocation and Hybrid Matrix Factorization with SVD++ and ALS
authors: Mbarek Marwan, Hind Ait Temghart, Safae Hmaidi, Mohamed Lazaar
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Intelligent edge caching has emerged as a promising solution for supporting next-generation data centers by improving content delivery. However, the increasing complexity of deep neural network architectures often results in significant computational overhead and increased service latency. To overcome these limitations, this study introduces a lightweight hybrid matrix factorization framework that combines Singular Value Decomposition (SVD++) and Alternating Least Squares (ALS) based on the Bates–Granger (BG) weighting scheme. Furthermore, Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) is employed for topic-based content clustering, enabling a better trade-off between recommendation accuracy and computational efficiency. The simulation results, statistically validated using the Friedman test followed by the Nemenyi post-hoc analysis, demonstrate that the proposed model consistently outperforms competing approaches. Specifically, the hybrid model achieves accuracy improvements of 20–30% on the dense Amazon Reviews dataset and 4–10% on the sparse Book-Crossing dataset compared with both classical and neural baseline models. In addition, clustering improves computational efficiency by reducing training time by 10–42% compared with models such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Overall, the results indicate that the proposed proactive caching technique achieves a better balance between accuracy and efficiency, while cross-validation confirms its robustness and strong generalization capability across different data partitions.

title: IEEE Moving
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Microwave Magazine

title: A Unified Intelligence Framework for Early Liver Disease Prediction: Bridging Interpretability and Diagnostic Precision
authors: G. Sajiv, N. Meenakshisundaram
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Sustainable Computing and Smart Systems (ICSCSS)
abstract: Many liver diseases are a global health problem, with symptoms sometimes not appearing until the disease is very serious. intervention and better patient results depend on making early and correct predictions. This research presents a Unified Intelligence Framework for predicting liver disease by combining several machine learning classifiers into a single model. This framework splits the work between individual base learners, who spot different trends in the data and a meta-learner which combines what they have discovered for the best prediction. By looking at mutual information and doing correlation analysis, only clinically pertinent variables such as bilirubin, liver enzymes and albumin-globulin ratio were retained. The ensemble model achieved both a high accuracy of 89.70% and an AUC of 0.94, exceeding individual models and common traditional baselines. Detailed testing revealed that mistakes were reduced when looking for critical illnesses. This study proves that AI works best when its predictions are easy to relate to which helps build dependable AI-assisted diagnostic systems. It is possible that the framework can be applied usefully in clinical decision making in hepatology.

title: Finite-Element Framework for Electrostatics and Optical Simulations in IGZO TFTs—Part I: Modeling
authors: Pietro Rinaudo, Adrian Chasin, Ben Kaczer, Ying Zhao, Harold Dekkers, Subhali Subhechha, Attilio Belmonte, Valeri Afanas'ev, Ingrid De Wolf, Jacopo Franco
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: We present a finite-element-based 1-D formalism to solve the Poisson equation for a thin-film transistor with a floating body. We apply our framework to InGaZnO (IGZO)-based devices, taking into account its subgap density of states (DoS), revealing its impact on device electrostatics, which is fundamental for predicting device performance and reliability. Our simple 1-D approach yields identical results as the more computationally intensive 2-D TCAD implementations, and it enables the convenient implementation of physics-based IGZO-specific models. Finally, we apply the new framework to quantify the IGZO deep subgap DoS measured by light-assisted <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$I$</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V$</tex-math> </inline-formula> spectroscopy.

title: Intelligent MPPT-Driven Modelling of SOFC Systems with DC–DC Boost Conversion
authors: Priya M.S., Saravanan S.
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFCs) deliver clean energy conversion and high efficiency, but require a smart controller to perform at the maximum power point (MPP) under dynamic load and thermal conditions. This work presents an Artificial Neural System (ANN) based MPPT algorithm for a 3 kW SOFC with a boost converter. Unlike conventional methods like Perturb and Observe (P&O), the proposed work predicts the optimal duty cycle using inputs such as stack voltage and current. Trained on data from a validated MATLAB/Simulink SOFC model, the ANN-MPPT attains rapid convergence in less than 0.2 seconds with no steady-state oscillations. Results show stable output power of 2940 W, a steady current of 10.5 A, a regulated voltage of 280 V, and an efficiency of 95.4%, confirming superior accuracy, stability, and efficiency. This work establishes the ANN-MPPT as a reliable, adaptable solution for high-performance SOFC power systems.

title: Ensemble Graph Neural Networks with Individual Decision Feedback for Graph Classification
authors: Mingjian Guang, Zhong Li, Rui Zhang, Junli Wang, Dawei Cheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Graph classification is a classic data mining task in graph-related domains. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as essential methods for graph classification due to their powerful ability to extract knowledge from graph-level data. Most methods employ a single GNN to learn graph representations and may overfit small datasets. Some ensemble graph neural networks combine multiple independent GNNs as an ensemble classifier, leveraging collective intelligence to provide more robust collective decisions to alleviate overfitting. Despite their effectiveness, they hardly consider and analyze the underlying implications behind the consistency and inconsistency of individual decisions. Moreover, concrete ensemble learning strategies for diverse individual decisions are currently lacking. Therefore, we propose an ensemble graph neural network with individual decision feedback that efficiently combines multiple GNNs for graph classification. In particular, we conduct a detailed analysis of the potential information fed back from individual decisions and propose an individual decision feedback weighting method to dynamically adjust model training, encouraging GNNs to focus more on the crucial graphs for training. Besides, we design a credibility-evaluation-based decision fusion method to fuse the most reliable individual decisions as the collective decision. Finally, we evaluate the performance of our method by conducting extensive experiments and demonstrating its effectiveness.

title: TAFSNet: A Type-Aware Low-Frequency Semantic Consistency Network for Robust Deepfake Detection
authors: Yuxin Shui, Jing Wu
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI in Education Technology and Applications (AIETA)
abstract: DeepFake detectors often degrade in open-world deployment, where forgeries are produced by unseen generators and test data suffer from domain shift. While frequency-space decoupling can improve transferability, fixed band partitioning introduces spectrum bias across heterogeneous manipulations, and low-quality cross-domain data may distort low-frequency semantics, encouraging spurious domain shortcuts. We propose TAFSNet with two components: (i) Type-Adaptive Frequency Attention, a lightweight type-conditioned routing that reweights frequency bands per input to capture type-specific spectral anomalies, and (ii) Low-Frequency Semantic Consistency regularization that aligns low-frequency content between synthesized faces and real backgrounds to suppress semantic drift. Experiments on standard DeepFake benchmarks under severe perturbations show consistent improvements over prior methods with minimal overhead, especially in cross-domain evaluation.
